text_snippet: China Air Force Magazine Articles on Master Chief Program The August 2015 issue of China Air Force (中国空军) magazine had 22 pages of articles concerning the test case program for the PLAAF’s Master Chief program under the overall title “Air Force Master Chief” (空军士官长). Page 16: In June 2015, a new Master Chief with the rank of Master Sergeant Class-2 (二级军士长), Liang Wan (梁万), was assigned to a Shijiazhuang Flight Academy Training Brigade in a management billet (管理岗位) and received his own office. He served as a conscript starting in 1997. His background was an aircraft maintenance technician. In his new position, he focuses more on the management and training of soldiers throughout the brigade, and can even participate [as a member] in the decision-making of the brigade Party Committee (在全新的岗位上,他将更多的精力集中到全旅的士兵管理,训练,甚至还可能参与旅党委决策). As part of the new Master Chief system (士官长制度), in June 2015, he was one of part of the first group of Master Chiefs assigned in management billets to about 10 brigade and regiment test points. Overall, the PLAAF started training 262 Master Chiefs at the brigade, regiment, battalion, company, and platoon levels.  Previously, the highest position an NCO could serve in was a squad leader (班长) or as an “acting” platoon commander (代理排长). [They were considered “acting” because they could not hold an officer’s grade.] Since 1997, he served under seven different company commanders. For his brigade, 12 were selected out of the original 43 who began the program. The 12 covered all levels within the brigade, not just the headquarters. However, six personnel competed for the billet within the brigade headquarters, which Liang was chosen.  Some NCOs were selected for airborne regiments and their subordinate battalions and companies. The Master Chief for one airborne company is a Sergeant First Class (上士) NCO. One Master Chief is assigned to a Beijing MRAF SAM regiment’s battalion. Key Vocab:
士官 - non-commissioned ofﬁcer(s); NCO(s) 
军士长 - Master Sergeant(s) (Senior NCOs)
士兵 - enlisted (soldiers; or, soldiers and NCOs)

志愿兵 - volunteer soldiers
合同兵 - contract soldiers

Key Notes:
-
In Chinese, PLA enlisted rank titles are uniﬁed across services 
(Tri-Service; 三军, or joint 联合), without service speciﬁc titles. 
 All PLA ranks are based on traditional ground forces.  
-
All PLAA soldiers, PLAN sailors, PLAAF airman, PLARF 
rocketeers, PLASSF soldiers, and PAP armed police, active, 
reserve and militia are “soldiers” (士兵) and “sergeants” (士官).
-
There are no “petty ofﬁcers”, “sailors”, “marines”, or “airman”, etc.

NCO History  The 1988 “Regulations of the Chinese People’s Liberation Army on the Active-Duty Service of Enlisted Personnel” (zhongguo renmin jiefangjun xianyi shibing fuyi tiaoli; 中国人民解放军现役士兵服役条例) established the system of preferential treatment for both conscripts and volunteer soldiers, and contained stipulations governing their work arrangements following demobilization. These regulations also reestablished the system of grades and ranks for the enlisted force. Soldiers serving as volunteers in the PLA became known as noncommissioned officers (shiguan; 士官) and were accorded the rank of either sergeant (junshi; 军士) or specialist sergeant (zhuanye junshi; 专业军士).   Second Enlisted Rank System: 1988-1999 When the PLA reinstated ranks in 1988, the enlisted ranks were reorganized into three grades and nine levels. Depending on the PLA source document, the first conscript grade was identified as bing (兵) or shibing (士兵) and had two rank levels.  Some PLA sources list the next grade, identified as junshi (军士), as part of the conscript system (yiwubing yizhi; 义务兵役制), while others list it as part of the volunteer system (zhiyuanbing yizhi; 志愿兵役制). However, all sources note that this grade had three rank levels. The third grade, which all sources note as part of the volunteer system, was identified as shiguan (士官) and was divided into co-equal ranks of junshizhang (军士长) and zhuanye junshizhang (专业军士长). Each of these ranks had four levels, from grade 1 to grade 4. The present system was instituted in July 1999 with the revised “Regulations of the Chinese People’s Liberation Army on the Active-Duty Service of Enlisted Personnel” (zhongguo renmin jiefangjun xianyi shibing fuyi tiaoli; 中国人民解放军现役士兵服役条例). The PLA enlisted ranks between 1998 and 1999 are listed in Table 3 below and shown in Figure 7. United Nations Issues: U.S. Funding of U.N. Peacekeeping 
The United States is the single largest financial contributor 
to United Nations (U.N.) peacekeeping activities. Congress 
authorizes and appropriates U.S. contributions, and it has an 
ongoing interest in ensuring such funding is used as 
efficiently and effectively as possible. The United States, as 
a permanent member of the U.N. Security Council, plays a 
key role in establishing, renewing, and funding U.N. 
peacekeeping operations. For 2021, the United Nations 
assessed the U.S. share of U.N. peacekeeping budgets at 
27.89%; however, since 1994 Congress has capped the U.S. 
payment at 25% due to concerns that U.S. assessments are 
too high. Congress appropriated $1.45 billion in 
contributions to most U.N. peacekeeping activities for 
FY2021 (up to the 25% cap). 
U.N. Peacekeeping Funding 
The United Nations currently operates 13 U.N. 
peacekeeping missions worldwide, with more than 80,000 
military, police, and civilian personnel from over 100 
countries. The Security Council adopts a resolution to 
establish each operation and specifies how it will be funded. 
Historically, the Council has authorized the U.N. General 
Assembly to create a separate assessed account for each 
operation to be supported by member states contributions. 
In recent years, due to concerns about budget shortfalls, the 
Generally Assembly has temporarily allowed peacekeeping 
funding to be pooled for increased financial flexibility.   
The General Assembly adopts the scale of assessments for 
U.N. member contributions to peacekeeping operations 
every three years. The peacekeeping scale is based on a 
modification of the U.N. regular budget scale, with the five 
permanent council members assessed at a higher level than 
for the regular budget. For example, the United States is 
assessed at 22% of the regular budget; however, its current 
peacekeeping assessment is 27.8

The PLA’s Military Diplomacy under COVID-19  Kenneth W. Allen  In the 1950s, military cooperation was limited almost entirely to communist nations and insurgent movements in Southeast Asia. In the late 1970s, the scope and tenor of China’s foreign military cooperation changed with the shift to commercial arms sales, attempts to gain some influence in Eastern Europe, and improvement in relations with the United States (U.S.) and Western Europe. By the 1980s, China had also developed close military ties with Egypt, Tanzania, Sudan, Somalia, Zaire, and Zambia in Africa. The change reflected China’s desire to counter Soviet influence, especially in Europe, as well as to develop relations with modern armed forces. Chinese military ties with West European countries were strongest with Britain, France, and Italy. Chinese military relations with the U.S. developed rapidly in the 1980s and included exchanges of high-level military officials and working-level delegations in training, logistics, and education, as well as four foreign military sales (FMS) projects.  he PLA Navy (PLAN) did not make its first foreign port call until 1985, when it visited Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Bangladesh. Its first port call visit to the U.S. (Hawaii) was in 1989. Since 2008, the PLAN has deployed 38 escort task forces (ETF) to the Gulf of Aden. Until COVID, each ETF averaged 4-6 months at sea from the time they departed until they returned and spent an average of three months conducting their Gulf of Aden escort duties. They then conducted port calls on the way home. However, since COVID began, none have conducted port calls. The PLA began conducting bilateral and multilateral “combined exercises” with other countries in 2002 first became involved in United Nations Peacekeeping Operations (UNPKO) in 1990, when it sent five military observers to the UN Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO). text_snippet: Sri Lanka Reverses Decision Permitting China's Naval Vessel to Visit Note COVID supplies delivery earlier.   https://maritime-executive.com/article/sri-lanka-reverses-decision-permitting-china-s-naval-vessel-to-visit Published Aug 14, 2022 The controversial Chinese vessel Yuan Wang 5, which Western analysts believe is a spy ship designed to track and listen in on satellites and support intercontinental ballistic missile tests, will be permitted to dock in Sri Lanka this week. The planned stop, which the Chinese are officially labeling a “replenishment visit,” is causing tensions between India and China with suggestions that China is taking advantage of Sri Lanka’s current financial and political crisis. In a written statement issued on Saturday, August 13, Sri Lanka’s Foreign Affairs Ministry said, "Having considered all material in place, the clearance to the Embassy of the People's Republic of China was conveyed for the deferred arrival of the vessel from 16-22 August 2022." The Ministry reports that China had made the formal request in June for assistance while the vessel stopped at the Chinese run-Hambantota Port on the southern coast of the island nation followed up by a new request on August 12. Last week, the same ministry announced that it was asking China to indefinitely defer the controversial visit of the vessel which China described as “a survey and observation ship” believe to have a crew of 400 aboard. Tracking services and international observers report the vessel has been lingering in the Indian Ocean about 700 miles southeast of Sri Lanka and India in recent weeks. It passed through Indonesia waters and has been believed to have been recently circling in the region between Indonesia and Sri Lanka. Summary China’s military diplomacy plays a crucial role in advancing the nation's foreign policy objectives and safeguarding its strategic interests. The report highlights a diverse array of activities within military diplomacy, including senior-level meetings, joint military exercises, naval port calls, UN peacekeeping operations, and academic exchanges. Our findings reveal a significant recovery in China's military diplomacy activities in 2023, following a period of reduced contacts with foreign militaries. Southeast Asia and Russia remain primary partners for China, with emerging strategic importance also seen in Africa, Oceania, and the Middle East. Overall, China's military diplomacy underscores its efforts to diversify partnerships and assert its global influence, emphasizing regional stability and international cooperation in pursuit of its diplomatic goals. Introduction During their San Francisco meeting in November 2023, Chinese and U.S. statesmen agreed to resume high-level military-to-military communications, paving the way to the first talk between their top defense officials in over a year. While China almost froze its military diplomacy with the United States in 2023, the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) was busy enhancing its cooperation with other foreign militaries. According to the PLA, “military diplomacy is an important component of a country’s foreign affairs, and it can even be considered the ‘ballast stone’(压舱石) of a nation’s diplomacy.” Thus, tracking the PLA’s diplomatic activities can help us better understand trends in China’s overall diplomatic goals and interests.  This report will review China’s military diplomacy activities in 2023, including senior-level visits and meetings, joint exercises, naval port calls, and humanitarian activities. 中越海警举行第七次高级别工作会晤暨越南海警8002舰来访活动 来源：解放军报 作者：张鑫 黄祖鹏 责任编辑：刘上靖 2023-12-09 14:19:08 新华社广州12月8日电 （张鑫、黄祖鹏）12月4日至8日，中国海警局与越南海警司令部在广州举行第七次高级别工作会晤暨越南海警8002舰来访活动。 会上，双方积极评价了年内合作成果，讨论了下步合作方向和具体合作项目，就共同关心的话题交换了意见。双方一致表示要进一步巩固两国传统友谊，深化海上务实合作，稳妥处置海上突发事件，共同维护海上安全稳定。会后，越南海警代表团参观访问了中国海警局驻广州、上海机构。 越南海警8002舰于12月4日至8日应邀访问广州。访问期间，双方举行了码头迎送仪式、文体交流、互登舰艇参观、舰艇专业技术交流、甲板招待会等系列活动。  http://www.mod.gov.cn/gfbw/jswj/lf/16263691.html 张又侠会见苏里南国防部长 来源：国防部网 责任编辑：贺书引 2023-11-01 12:42:21 中央军委副主席张又侠11月1日在京会见来华出席第十届北京香山论坛的苏里南国防部长马图拉。 张又侠说，中苏关系长期处于中国同加勒比国家关系前列，堪称国与国之间友好相处、平等相待的典范。在习近平主席和单多吉总统战略引领下，两国政治互信不断增强，互利合作持续拓展，人文交流日益深化。两军关系发展势头良好，团组互访不断，人员培训交流稳步推进，各领域务实合作取得一系列成果。中方愿与苏方一道，强化战略沟通，扩展务实合作，推动两军关系高质量发展。 马图拉祝贺第十届北京香山论坛成功举办。她说，苏里南和中国有着长期的友好关系。苏方感谢中方的帮助与支持，愿与中国军队共同努力，将两军关系提升到新高度。 http://www.mod.gov.cn/gfbw/jswj/lf/16263544.html 何卫东同出席第十届北京香山论坛客人会见会谈 来源：国防部网 责任编辑：李庆桐 2023-10-31 22:51:43 中央军委副主席何卫东31日在京分别会见来华出席第十届北京香山论坛的尼日利亚国防部长阿布巴卡尔、阿塞拜疆国防部长加桑诺夫、新加坡国防部长黄永宏，并同柬埔寨副首相兼国防大臣迪西哈举行会谈。 在会见阿布巴卡尔时，何卫东说，尼是中国在非洲的重要战略伙伴。建交以来，两国始终相互尊重、互惠互利，在涉及彼此核心利益问题上相互支持，在国际和地区事务中密切配合。希望双方在两国领导人战略引领下，加强战略协调，共享发展机遇，深挖合作潜力，携手将中尼关系打造成中非合作的典范。中国军队愿与尼方保持两军关系良好发展势头，继续密切各层级沟通，推动两军合作不断迈上新台阶。 阿布巴卡尔说，尼中友好交往密切而真诚，在高层往来、人员培训等方面取得丰硕成果，尼方坚定支持中国在国际和地区事务中的原则立场。 在会见加桑诺夫时，何卫东说，中阿有着绵延千年的深厚情谊。建交以来，两国始终相互尊重、互利共赢，政治互信不断深化，务实合作成果丰硕。中方高度赞赏阿方积极响应构建人类命运共同体理念和参与共建“一带一路”。中国军队愿与阿方落实好两国元首重要共识，保持两军关系发展向上向好势头，密切高层交往，增进战略互信，拓展人员合作，努力取得更多新成果。 加桑诺夫说，阿方坚定奉行一个中国原则，一贯高度重视对华关系，愿与中方加强包括共建“一带一路”在内的各领域合作，发展互利互惠的两国两军关系。 在会见黄永宏时，何卫东说，中新两国长期以来相互借鉴、相互支持，友好合作成果丰硕。中方愿同新方一道，深化落实两国领导人达成的重要共识，共乘和平发展快车，保持良好合作势头，坚决反对霸道霸凌，共同抵制“脱钩断链”，更好造福两国和地区人民。希望中新两军进一步增进互信、深化合作，加强多边协调配合，为维护南海地区和平稳定贡献力量。 黄永宏积极评价北京香山论坛地位作用，表示新方愿与中国军队拓展人员往来、联演联训等领域交流合作，不断提升新中防务合作水平，共同应对面临的安全风险和挑战。 在同迪西哈会谈时，何卫东说，中柬是铁杆朋友。习近平主席今年先后同前首相洪森、新任首相洪玛奈举行会谈，就构建高质量、高水平、高标准的新时代中柬命运共同体达成重要共识，引领中柬友谊不断向前发展。希望双方共享机遇、共谋合作、共促发展，推动中柬关系好上加好、更上层楼。两军应传承发扬好中柬传统友谊，继续加强战略

Military Exchanges with Chinese Characteristics: The PLA Experience with Military Relations   Heidi Holz and Kenneth Allen CNA China Studies    Heidi Holz is a China analyst at CNA China Studies. Prior to joining CNA, she worked as a bio defense analyst and China specialist with the Division of Integrated Bio Defense at Georgetown University’s Imaging Science and Information Systems Center, where she analyzed the frequency and causality of infectious diseases in various parts of China. An accomplished linguist, Ms. Holz was previously a staff member at the Voice of America’s Mandarin Service, where she wrote programming in both Chinese and English, to include broadcast programs for teaching English to VOA’s Chinese audience. Ms. Holz has studied Mandarin Chinese in Beijing at Peking University and Tsinghua University. She is a graduate of Georgetown University’s School of Foreign Service where she studied Chinese and East Asian history. She is currently completing a master’s degree in Security Studies at Georgetown University.   Kenneth Allen is a Senior China Analyst at CNA China Studies. He is a retired U.S. Air Force officer with many years of analytic work on China. His extensive service abroad includes a tour in China as the Assistant Air Attaché, plus assignments in Taiwan, Japan, and Berlin. A Chinese linguist, he holds an MA in International Relations from Boston University. He is the author of innumerable articles and three major monographs on Chinese military affairs, including China's Foreign Military Relations. Allen authored the seminal English-language monograph on the PLA Air Force that remains the cornerstone for research on the Chinese Air Force. Training Vessel Qi Jiguang PLA Navy Training Ship Qi Jiguang As part of the PLA’s Annual Plan for International Cooperation, the PLAN’s training ship Qi Jiguang (Hull 83), which was commissioned in 2017 and is assigned to the Dalian Naval Academy, conducted two training voyages during 2023.  The first voyage lasted for 41 days from May 15 to June 24 with 476 naval cadets and sailors onboard to conduct cadet training in far seas and pay 3-5 day visits to four countries, including Vietnam, Thailand (5th visit), Brunei (2nd visit), and the Philippines (1st visit). That voyage, which sailed more than 8,500 nautical miles, marked the training ship’s third navigation training and visit mission in open seas since its commission in 2017. More than 6,000 people visited the ship, including the Chinese Ambassador and military attaches assigned to each country, as well as key naval officers from the host country. The cadets also visited naval facilities, museums, and academies. Besides finishing a series of training programs, the vessel held joint exercises with navy vessels from Vietnam, Brunei, and the Philippines. The second voyage lasted for 49 days from September 4 to October 23 with over 300 cadets from the PLA Naval University of Engineering, Naval Submarine Academy, and Naval Aviation University onboard. It also included 10 midshipmen from Pakistan, Thailand, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, and Fiji. The ship visited Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, and Fiji. Introduction to the PLA’s Organizational Reforms: 2000-2012 
Kenneth W. Allen 
The active components of the PLA can be categorized into the general 
headquarters/departments, the services and arms, and the military area 
commands. The PLA’s general headquarters/departments system is composed of 
the General Staff Headquarters, the General Political Department, the General 
Logistics Department, and the General Armaments Department, which are placed 
under the leadership of the CMC. The CMC, through these four general 
headquarters/departments, directs and commands all the military area commands 
and the services and arms. The routine work of the Ministry of National Defense 
is handled, respectively, by these four general headquarters/departments.  
The PLA is composed of three services - the Army (PLAA), Navy (PLAN) and Air 
Force (PLAAF)- and an independent arm, the Second Artillery Force (PLASAF). 
The Army has such arms as the infantry, artillery, armor, engineering, 
communications, anti-chemical warfare and Army aviation, as well as other 
specialized units. The Navy has such arms as the surface, submarine, naval 
aviation, coastal defense and marine corps, as well as other specialized units. The 
Air Force has such arms as the aviation, surface-to-air missile and antiaircraft 
artillery, radar, and airborne, as well as other specialized units. The Second 
Artillery Force is composed of the strategic missile, conventional missile, and 
other specialized units.  
The military area commands (theaters of war) of the PLA are military 
organizations set up according to the state’s administrative divisions, 
geographical locations, strategic and operational orientations, and operational 
tasks. Under each military area command are a number of Army combined corps, 
units of various arms, logistical support units, and provincial or garrison

Chapter Two: The Central Military Commission  Alice Miller  The Central Military Commission (CMC/中央军事委员会/中央军委) is the apex of the military hierarchy in China’s political order.  Under the 1982 constitution of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), a party CMC is appointed by the party Central Committee at the same plenum that appoints the party Politburo and its Standing Committee, the Secretariat, and the Central Discipline Inspection Commission leadership. Under the 1982 People’s Republic of China (PRC) constitution, a state CMC is appointed by the National People’s Congress (NPC) and is institutionally co-equal with the PRC’s executive branch, the State Council.  In China’s military hierarchy, the CMC is the top grade body that outranks every other military institution.    Most studies of the CMC focus on its mission, organization and internal processes, and relationship with other institutions in China’s military sector. As critically important as this focus is, it nevertheless suffers from what Mao Zedong in 1929 called a “purely military viewpoint” that neglects the broader political context that a military serves. The CMC’s role, organization, and processes are better understood from two perspectives: first, from the perspective of the broader hierarchies of civilian political institutions within which it fits; and second, from the perspective of the military sector itself. This paper offers assessments from both perspectives since 2000.  The CMC in the Broader Political Order The authority and place of the CMC in China’s political order today reflect the maturation of fundamental changes in the overall institutional structure and processes of politics made in the early 1980s under the leadership of Deng Xiaoping. Section 3: Organizational Status  When examining Second Artillery the first issues to address are 1) what is the proper English name, 2) what is its organizational relationship to the PLA Army (PLAA), Navy (PLAN), and Air Force (PLAAF) and where does Second Artillery fit into the PLA’s 15-grade structure? Specifically, is the PLASAF a service (军种) or an independent branch (独立兵种) and should the U.S. government refer to it as Second Artillery Corps with the acronym SAC or Second Artillery Force with the acronym PLASAF?  Although perhaps seemingly trivial matters to one unfamiliar with the PLA, it is important to clarify these issues. Foreigners writing on the Second Artillery often use different English names, and it would be useful to have a standard English translation based on authoritative PLA sources, at least within the US Government and Department of Defense. Additionally, it is not uncommon for foreign analysts of the PLA to refer to the “four services” strictly for the sake of simplicity; however, this is not correct. Although many analysts and people who write about Second Artillery focus primarily on its strategy and weapons, it is also important to understand exactly where Second Artillery fits within the PLA’s overall structure.  What is the Proper English Name? Since at least 1997, the PLA has consistently used Second Artillery and the Second Artillery Force as the official English name. No earlier English language references were found. In the official white paper on China’s National Defense published in 2010, the PLA added the acronym PLASAF. The Ministry of National Defense’s (MND) official website also uses the acronym SAF. On the other hand, from 1997 through 2012, the U.S. Department of Defense (DOD) Annual Report to Congress on Military and Security Developments Involving the People’s Republic of China used Second Artillery Corps and added the acronym SAC in 2011. In 2013, the report began using Second Artillery Force but has yet to add the PLASAF

Important as the development of the regular PLA has been to China’s emergence as 
a global power, China’s ability to develop effective, professional paramilitary 
internal security and emergency response forces will almost certainly have a greater 
impact on China’s day-to-day national security than any other effort to strengthen 
China’s military and security forces.  The Chinese Communist Party continues to 
face increasing social unrest, violent crime, cross-border drug trafficking, and other 
threats to internal stability. Moreover, it has been made clear by recent major social 
uprisings and natural disasters, such as the 2008 and 2009 riots in Lhasa and 
Urumqi, and the 2008 Sichuan earthquake, that when China’s paramilitary and 
emergency response forces are unable to handle these crises, Beijing has little choice 
but to call upon the regular PLA help support these disaster relief and domestic 
security operations. China’s capacity to develop emergency response and 
paramilitary forces, therefore, will continue to have a major impact on the regular 
PLA’s ability to free itself to focus on its new emerging missions.

text_snippet: PLA Air Force Organizational Structure Kenneth W. Allen Concerning the PLA Air Force organizational structure, the Air Force practices a leadership system that combines operational command with Air Force building and management. The organizational system consists of Air Force Headquarters, seven Military Region Air Force (MRAF) Headquarters, [deputy] corps- and division-level command posts (CPs), divisions, brigades, and regiments. The Air Force four branches—aviation, surface-to-air missile (SAM), antiaircraft artillery (AAA), and airborne—plus five types of specialty forces—communications, radar, electronic countermeasures (ECM), chemical defense, and technical reconnaissance. The Air Force also has education, research, testing, and training institutions. China’s National Defense The purpose of this paper is to examine the organizational structure (体制编制) of the People’s Liberation Army Air Force (PLAAF) (人民解放军空军) based on the following three questions, each of which are addressed in separate sections: Section 1: What is the PLAAF’s current organizational structure and what are the historical, theoretical, bureaucratic, and other reasons for the current structure? Section 2: What are the implications of the current organizational structure for the PLAAF’s future development? Section 3: How might the PLAAF’s organizational structure change in order to operate in a joint conflict? This paper does not discuss the PLAAF’s order of battle, which is covered adequately in other reports, such as the US Department of Defense’s annual report on the People’s Liberation Army (PLA).  Introduction During the 1990s, the PLAAF began purchasing high-tech weapons from abroad, as well as developing and purchasing them domestically, including combat aircraft (Su-27s), SAMs (SA-10s), and radar and ECM systems that have now formed the cornerstone of its table of organization and equipment (TO&E). Allen Senior Analyst, TASC  Most analysis of China’s aviation focuses on hardware and order-of-battle issues – how many aircraft of what types with what capabilities were produced, where are they located, and what systems are under development.  While most analysis of strategy and doctrine concentrates on the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) as a whole, there is also interest in strategy and doctrine for the aviation forces.  During a seminar on Airpower in the Asia-Pacific Region I hosted in 1999, a former USAF Chief of Staff provided the following list of priority questions that he would want to know about China’s airpower:  What is their battle doctrine? Is their organizational direction defensive or offensive? Which classic air missions do they cover effectively air defense, counter-air, conventional strike? What is their basic employment concept for these air missions manned aircraft, ground or air launched missiles? What kind of support packages do they have defense suppression, air escort, tanker support? What kind of power projection capability does their equipment represent range, payload? How modern is their capability in precision guided weapons and battlespace surveillance? What is their equipment and personnel readiness status? What size, duration, and location of operations can they sustain with logistics support?  Most of this information, which is extremely valuable to war planners, can be ascertained by carefully analyzing intelligence information on existing equipment capabilities, weapons systems under development, deployment patterns, and the types of training being conducted. While continuing to attach importance to building the Army, the PLA gives priority to building the Navy, Air Force, and Second Artillery Force to seek balanced development of the combat force structure, to strengthen the capabilities for winning both command of the sea and command of the air, and to conduct strategic counter-strikes. The PLA Air Force is responsible for safeguarding China’s airspace security and maintaining a stable air defense posture nationwide. To meet the requirements of informationalized air operations, the Air Force has gradually shifted from one of territorial air defense to one of both offensive and defensive operations. Emphasis is placed on the development of new fighters, air defense and anti-missile weapons, and the means of information operations and automated command systems. Combined arms and multi-role aircraft combat training is intensified to improve the capabilities in operations such as air strikes, air defense, information counter-measures, early warning and reconnaissance, strategic mobility, and integrated support. This handbook is based on information compiled from open source Chinese publications, as well as discus­
sions with Chinese military officials and US academicians.  The intent of the study is to educate US Air Force 
members about China’s People’s Liberation Army (PLA) Air Force (PLAAF) beyond weapon systems, equip­
ment, and order of battle information. The handbook focuses on the PLAAF’s history, leadership, organiza­
tional structure, political work system, personnel, education, training, logistics and maintenance, quality-of-life, 
and foreign relations. It also discusses each of the PLAAF’s four branches—aviation, surface-to-air missile, 
antiaircraft artillery, and airborne, but does not provide information on specific weapon systems and equip­
ment. This handbook complements the Office of Naval Intelligence’s handbook on the PLA Navy China’s Navy 
2007, and Dennis Blasko’s The Chinese Army Today, on the PLA’s ground forces.
Much like the PLA as a whole, the PLAAF has been undergoing transformational change over the past decade, 
transforming itself from a poorly equipped and trained organization into an increasingly capable fighting force. 
Dramatic changes have occurred, and continue to occur, in the areas of mission, organizational structure, per­
sonnel, education, training, and equipment.
Transformation in the PLAAF began with a change in mission and expectations; from a force focused on ter­
ritorial air defense, to a force with growing regional strike missions and capabilities, and long-term expectations 
of being able to perform extra-regional missions supporting PRC national objectives.
To help accomplish this growing mission set, the PLAAF has focused on increasing the education and training 
levels of its officer corps and enlisted force.  The PLAAF has become a much more professional force, still 
working to deal with monumental organizational, cultural and hardware changes.

PLAAF IADS Issues Ken Allen, 19 November 2019 Based on a request from someone, below are my thoughts concerning the PLA Air Force’s (PLAAF) Integrated Air Defense System (IADS) issues. It is merely based on my personal analysis of information I have gathered for the past 20 years.  Components The basic components consist of the following organizations and systems: Central Military Commission’s Joint Operations Command Center (JOCC) 4 Service HQ (Army/PLAA, Navy/PLAN, PLAAF, Rocket Force/PLAARF) and Strategic Support Force (PLASSF) HQ Command Posts (CP) 5 Theater Command (TC) JOCCs (Eastern, Southern, Western, Northern, and Central) 5 TC Army (TCA) and TC Air Force (TCAF) and 3 TC Navy (TCN) CPs Corps-level bases CPs Satellites Technical reconnaissance units Reconnaissance aircraft units Combat aircraft units Radar units SAM units Intelligence Collection Satellites are used to collect SIGINT (COMINT and ELINT) and IMINT and as communications relay platforms. Over-the-horizon radars (OTHR) and technical reconnaissance units also collect relevant information. Once the information is collected, it is then passed to the following entities: The CMC’s JOCC in the Western Hills of Beijing. The Chief of the Joint Staff Department (JSD) is the head of the JOCC. One senior officer, including the deputy chiefs of the JSD, is always the duty officer. The JOCC is staffed by permanent duty officers from each of the four services and the PLA’s Strategic Support Force (PLASSF). The 4 Services’ (Army, Navy, Air Force, Rocket Force) Command Posts (CP), even though they, theoretically, are not in the operational chain of command since the reorganization began in 2015. The Chief of Staff (director of the Staff Department) is the head of the CPs. There is always one senior officer serving as the duty officer, which can include the commander, the deputy commanders, the chief of staff, and the deputy chiefs of staff. text_snippet: {TITLE}PLA Navy Personnel Recruitment, Education, and Training  Looking back at the parlous state of the People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) in the early 1980s, Liu Huaqing, its former commander, wrote, “All areas [of the navy] required significant strengthening, but I believed, the key was developing capable personnel.” Indeed, during Admiral Liu’s tenure (1982–88), the PLAN embarked on a major effort to improve the quality of its officers and enlisted personnel—an effort that continues to this day.  The purpose of this monograph is to examine the results of reforms in the way in which the PLAN recruits, educates, and trains its officers and enlisted personnel. Although these reforms began in the 1980s, they experienced a boost in the 1990s, when procurement of advanced weaponry (made possible by expanding national defense budgets) increased, creating an urgent need for a more highly educated force to operate, support, and maintain the new systems. To this end, the PLAN has gradually shifted from an officer force consisting primarily of graduates of PLAN academic institutions who were selected from high school graduates and outstanding enlisted personnel with high school educations to a combination of PLAN academic-institution graduates, officers who have graduated with science, technology, or engineering degrees from the newly created National Defense Student (Reserve Officer) Program, and directly recruited graduates of civilian college who have appropriate technical degrees. The PLAN is currently in the process of consolidating and reorganizing its officer academic institutions and professional military education (PME) structure to provide a continuum of education and training throughout an officer’s career.  Reforms in the enlisted force continue to lag behind those of the officer corps. text_snippet: PLA Navy Personnel Recruitment, Education, and Training Kenneth Allen and Morgan Clemens  This monograph discusses how the PLA Navy (PLAN) recruits its officer corps and enlisted force, how they receive their basic education and training, and how they receive their professional military education (PME) and individual specialty training as they move up the career ladder.  As the PLAN began acquiring more sophisticated weapons and equipment in the 1990s, it realized that it did not have a highly educated force of officers and enlisted personnel to operate, support, and maintain the systems. As a result, it has gradually shifted from an officer force consisting primarily of PLAN academic institution graduates who were selected from high school graduates and outstanding enlisted personnel with a high school education to a combination of PLAN academic institution graduates, officers who have graduated with a science, technology, or engineering degree from the newly created National Defense Student (Reserve Officer) Program, and directly recruited civilian college graduates with appropriate technical degrees. The PLAN is currently in the process of consolidating and reorganizing its officer academic institutions and professional military education (PME) structure to provide a continuum of education and training throughout an officer’s career.  The enlisted force continues to lag behind the reforms in the officer corps. In 1999, the PLAN, like the rest of the PLA, went from an enlisted force composed of four-year conscripts who could then remain on active duty for a total of 16 years as a volunteer to a two-year conscription period with the opportunity to remain on active duty for a total of 30 years as part of a newly-created noncommissioned officer (NCO) corps. The problem was that the force was still composed primarily of personnel with only a ninth grade education. text_snippet: PLA Convenes Work Conference on Training National Defense Students CPP20071214710010 Beijing Jiefangjun Bao (Internet Version-WWW) in Chinese 14 Dec 07 p 1 [Dispatch by reporters Li Xuanqing and Hu Chunhua: "Whole Military Sees Good Trend of Development in Relying on Ordinary Colleges, Universities To Cultivate National Defense Students"] Our military is seeing a strong trend of development in relying on ordinary colleges and students to cultivate national defense students. The Work Conference on the Training of National Defense Students released a piece of inspiring news: the number of colleges and universities to sign contracts with the military has increased from 21 eight years ago to the current 116; there are 48,000 national defense students on the campus and 80 percent of college graduates recruited by the military are sent to the frontline troops. An array of new-type, highly qualified talent composed of national defense students is taking shape in our military modernization. In order to accelerate the process of tackling the shortage of highly qualified new-type military talent, the party Central Committee, the State Council and the Central Military Commission made a strategic decision eight years ago to rely on national education to cultivate military cadres so as to gradually open up a road that unites military cultivation with relying on national education for cultivation and so as to select highly qualified talent from a wider range. Our military's program of the strategic project of highly qualified talent issued in 2003 expressly proposed relying on national education to cultivate at least 60 percent of military-cultivated cadres by 2010. A large number of key colleges and universities -- such as Beijing University, Qinghua University, and Fudan University -- took the lead in shouldering the task of cultivating national defense students. 特色；坚持教书与育人并重，成人与成才并举。坚持“立足海军，面向全军，服务社会”的办学理念，坚持“定向培养、定向就业”的办学方针，采用校企合作“订 单式”教育，全日制教学模式，以提升学生的专业技能和综合素质为目标，以毕业学生就业为目的。办学多年来为社会培养和输送了大批优秀人才，深受社会各界认 可和好评。 ◆办学特色 1、 教学管理特色： 执行中国人民解放军海军职工大学专业的教学计划，所有课程均由海军职工大学的教授、副教授、讲师来完成。学生可兼修日语、韩语。学生的毕业证书由国家教育 部电子注册。可在教育部学历证书网站上查询。学校和多所军队高校合作，完成本科学历教育，实现专、本同期毕业。学校下属的大连金凯恩教育发展有限公司负责 学生专业的职业资格认证工作。2009年被国际信息化认证协会授权“人才资格认证中心”；2010年成为“中物协会员单位”；2010年被辽宁省人力资源 和社会保障厅批准为“全国计算机高新技术考试基地”。 2、 学校管理特色： 学校实行军事化管理，每班配一名班主任，实行24小时跟踪服务，跟班管理，坚持“一切为了学生”的服务理念。通过耐心细致的思想工作和人性化管理，做到让 学生满意，让家长放心。培养学生具备良好的的军人素质和吃苦耐劳精神，养成遵纪守法、文明礼貌的行为规范，具备诚实守信、服从敬业、团结协作的团队意识， 提升学生的综合素质。 3、 就业安置特色： 学生入学即签订就业协议，学校让合格毕业生全部满意就业，并且跟踪服务三年。学校与两百余家企事业单位建有良好的用人供求合作关系，配有专门的就业办公室 负责学生就业，学生毕业，就业无忧。毕业生就业以大连地区为主，辐射北京、天津、上海、宁波、广州、深圳、青岛等沿海港口城市。航海类专业学生由学校下属 的德洋船务有限公司定点培训考证、定时上船实习和就业。 4、 奖励优惠特色： 学校设立一等奖学金：2000元；二等奖学金：1000元；三等奖学金：500元。 学校每年寒暑假为学生提供勤工俭学单位，两个月学生收入5000元左 右，学制的最后一年为带薪实习，能挣回前期所投入的费用。实行“零成本读大学”。在校学生符合国家规定条件可享受国家助学金补助，每年2400元，连续两 年。 ◆专业设置 一、物流管理 1、培养目标：主要培养物流师、报关员、报检员、市场营销师。从事熟练的报关、报检、单证、运输、仓储、配送、等物流业务操作与管理的高等技术应用型专门人才。 2、主要课程：物流管理、国贸实务、报关实务、商品编码、报检实务、专业英语、市场营销、计算机等。 3、资格证书：报关员、报检员、物流管理师、市场营销师、计算机等级证等 4、就业去向：面向以大连为主的报关行、外贸公司、货运公司、船务公司、制造企业、流通企业、服务企业和专业化的物流公司等。   二、建筑工程管理 1、培养目标：主要培养建筑工程施工员、质检员、预算员、资料员、监理员、安全员等。从事一般工程设计、工程项目管理、工程质量监理能力的中级管理专门人才。 2、主要课程：建筑施工技术、建筑识图与制图、建筑工程法律法规、建筑工程测量、建筑工程造价基础知识、建筑工程项目管理、建筑工程CAD、房屋建筑学、土木工程概论等。 3、资格证书：国家建设部门颁发的施工员、质检员、预算员、资料员、监理员、安全员等 text_snippet: Zheng He Training Ship Voyage in 2012 http://english.peopledaily.com.cn/90786/7792075.html   "Zheng He" training ship's round-the-world navigation boasts four features By Li Qinwei and Cao Jinping (PLA Daily)  Li Qinwei and Cao Jinping, “‘Zheng He’ training ship’s round-the-world navigation boasts four features,” PLA Daily in English, 19 April 2012 found at http://english.peopledaily.com.cn/90786/7792075.html April 19, 2012  The "Zheng He" oceangoing training ship, led by Rear Admiral Liao Shining, deputy chief of staff of the Navy of the Chinese People's Liberation Army (PLA), embarked on a glorious round-the-world voyage on April 16, 2012 from Dalian city, northeast China's Liaoning Province. Soon after the sailing, Liao Shining accepted the exclusive interview by the PLA Daily reporters with the ship and answered the questions of the objective and significance of the round-the-world sailing and the oceangoing practice and training for the Chinese and foreign naval cadets. Liao Shining said the "Harmonious Mission – the "Zheng He" ship's voyage around the world" boasts important significance on consolidating and developing the relations between China and various countries to be visited, promoting the exchanges between the Chinese and foreign navies, enhancing mutual trust, broadening the international perspective of the PLA naval officers and men, forging new PLA naval talents and showcasing the good image of the Chinese military. This is the second round-the-world sailing conducted by the PLA Navy after its first sailing by the No. 113 Ship Formation in 2002. Liao Shining briefed that this round-the-world sailing possesses four main features: First, the mission is independently carried out by the "Zheng He" ship which is an oceangoing sailing and training ship rather than a combatant ship or a fleet. Navy Professional Military Education In order to put the information concerning the PLA Navy’s officer and enlisted education and training system in perspective, it is important to begin by providing information about the U.S. Navy’s (USN) system.  U.S. Navy Officer PME According to the Joint Staff’s Officer Professional Military Education Policy (OPMEP) services operate officer professional military education (PME) systems to develop officers with expertise and knowledge appropriate to their grade, branch, and occupational specialty. Incorporated throughout Service-specific PME, officers receive joint professional military education (JPME) from precommissioning through general/flag officer level. Professional development is the product of a learning continuum that comprises training, experience, education, and self-improvement. PME provides the education needed to produce the most competent (strategic-minded, critical-thinking) individual possible. Training and education are not mutually exclusive. Virtually all military schools and professional development programs include elements of both education and training in their academic programs. The continuum structures the development of Service and joint officers by organizing the PME continuum into five military educational levels. It defines the focus of each level in terms of the major levels of war (tactical, operational, and strategic). The following levels are for the USN:  Precommissioning for cadets/midshipmen: Naval Academy, ROTC, OCS Primary for O-1s to O-3s: Branch, Warfare, and Staff Specialty Schools, and primary-level PME courses Intermediate for O-4s: College of Naval Command and Staff Senior for O-5s and O-6s: College of Naval Warfare Generals/Flag Officers for O-7s to O-9s: CAPSTONE, Joint Functions Component Commander Courses, SJIOAC, Joint Flat Officer Warfighting Course, PINNACLE. filename: PRC 1998-2019 Defense White Papers - PLAAF Content.docx
text_snippet: The Defense White Paper’s PLAAF Content Overview Ken Allen 24 July 2019: Overview In my view, one should look at all of the Defense White Papers (WPs) as a whole to examine the PLAAF. Specifically, each WP is not a stand-alone product that tells you everything you need to know, but there are still a lot of huge gaps, especially concerning weapon systems and equipment. As such, the WPs do not necessarily repeat information about the PLAAF that was already incorporated in earlier reports unless it has changed. That said, however, the PLAAF's public journal China Air Force (中国空军) is now published monthly instead of bimonthly, has increased from 80 pages to 100 pages and has lots of color photos covering each of the branches and specialty forces, and has become fairly revealing about many issues.  The first WP was published in 1998, but it was not until 2002 that the paper had a separate section covering the PLAAF’s history and structure. At that time, it was fairly revealing for an open publication. Whereas the 2002, 2004, 2006, and 2010 WPs each had one paragraph discussing the PLAAF, the 2008 had nine paragraphs covering history, organizational structure, personnel, education, and missions. This particular WP was quite helpful in trying to understand the PLAAF's organizational structure. In hindsight, the 2008 was the first document to indicate that the former SAM and AAA branches had been merged into a single ground air defense branch (地面防空兵), which it has also called the ground-to-air missile branch (2019). After reviewing the 2010 WP’s content, the following sentence is the only one that adds anything new. Specifically, this is the first mention of protecting the capital and the first time to mention MOOTW for the PLAAF, which is a direct result of its evacuation of Chinese citizens from Libya. Reform in China’s National Defense and Armed Forces V. Reasonable and Appropriate Defense Expenditure VI. Actively Contributing to Building a Community with a Shared Future for Mankind Closing Remarks Appendices Acronyms Preface Today, with their interests and security intertwined, people across the world are becoming members of a community with a shared future. China is at a critical stage of completing the building of a moderately prosperous society in all respects and embarking on the new journey of building a modernized socialist country in an all-round way. Socialism with Chinese characteristics has entered a new era. The Chinese government is issuing China’s National Defense in the New Era to expound on China’s defensive national defense policy and explain the practice, purposes and significance of China’s efforts to build a fortified national defense and a strong military, with a view to helping the international community better understand China’s national defense. I. International Security Situation The world today is undergoing profound changes unseen in a century. As economic globalization, the information society, and cultural diversification develop in an increasingly multi-polar world, peace, development and win-win cooperation remain the irreversible trends of the times. Nonetheless, there are prominent destabilizing factors and uncertainties in international security. The world is not yet a tranquil place. filename: PLA Theory and Doctrine in the 2019 White Paper.docx
text_snippet: PLA “Doctrine” and Theory A review of the 2000 to 2019 PRC Defense White Papers found several references to the English term “doctrine,” but the Chinese word was almost always lilun (理论), which normally translates as “theory” or “theoretical” throughout the defense white papers.   The 2009 version of US military JP 1-02 has the following definition for doctrine: Fundamental principles by which the military forces or elements thereof guide their actions in support of national objectives.  It is authoritative but requires judgment in application.  The 2019 version does not have a single definition for doctrine but does have the following definition for joint doctrine: Fundamental principles that guide the employment of United States military forces in coordinated action toward a common objective and may include terms, tactics, techniques, and procedures.  None of the JP 1-02 versions have the term theory in them.   The Chinese translate the US Army Training and Doctrine Command (TRADOC) as the US Army Training and Regulations Command (美国陆军训练与条令司令部)  2019 Defense White Paper The strategic goals for the development of China’s national defense and military in the new era are: • to comprehensively advance the modernization of military theory, organizational structure, military personnel, and weaponry and equipment in step with the modernization of the country and basically complete the modernization of national defense and the military by 2035; 新时代中国国防和军队建设的战略目标是，到2020年基本实现机械化，信息化建设取得重大进展，战略能力有大的提升。同国家现代化进程相一致，全面推进军事理论现代化、军队组织形态现代化、军事人员现代化、武器装备现代化，力争到2035年基本实现国防和军队现代化，到本世纪中叶把人民军队全面建成世界一流军队。 Promoting innovation in defense S&T and military theory. China’s armed forces are accelerating the implementation of the strategy to develop the military through S&T in a bid to maintain and enhance the strength of the areas where they lead, and intensify innovation in emerging areas. text_snippet: Revised national defense law to take effect in China on January 1, 2021 http://english.chinamil.com.cn/view/2020-12/29/content_9960152.htm Source China Military Online Editor Wang Xinjuan Time 2020-12-29 18:31:50 By Li Chun and Liang Xiaohui   BEIJING, December 29 -- The Standing Committee of the 13th National People’s Congress (NPC), China’s top legislature, adopted the revised National Defense Law at its 24th session on December 26. The revised law further embodies the character that China's national defense is a whole-of-people cause. It added contents about strengthening the national defense awareness and the crisis consciousness of all citizens, as well as improving the technology and capability level of national defense.   According to an official from the legislative bureau under China’s Central Military Commission (CMC), a total of 54 articles were revised, six were added and three deleted, and the titles of chapter four and five were changed. The revised National Defense Law, consisting of 73 articles under 12 chapters, will come into effect on January 1, 2021.   The revisions mainly cover 7 aspects.   First, the Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era is established as the guiding thought over China’s national defense activities.   Second, the national defense duties of relevant state organs are adjusted, and contents regarding the system wherein the CMC chairman shall assume overall responsibility are added.   Third, the tasks and goals for the construction of China’s armed forces are further elaborated.   Fourth, defense policy for major security domains is specified in view of activities and interest-defending needs in new-type security domains.   Fifth, the systems of national defense education and mobilization are enriched and perfected in consideration of the reforms in their leading and managing systems.   Sixth, protection of the position, rights, and interests of military personnel is intensified to make sure ser

It also makes China the world's largest economy. The European Union is second, at $22 trillion. The United States is third, producing $20.5 trillion.2﻿ China has 1.38 billion people, more than any other country in the world.3﻿ China remains a relatively poor country in terms of its standard of living. Its economy produces only $10,870 per person. In comparison, the U.S. gross domestic product per capita is $67,430.4﻿ China's low standard of living allows companies there to pay their workers less than what American workers earn. That makes products cheaper, which lures overseas manufacturers to outsource jobs to China. They then ship the finished goods to the United States, China’s largest trading partner. Components of China's Economy China built its economic growth on low-cost exports of machinery and equipment. Massive government spending went into state-owned companies to fuel those exports. These state-owned companies are less profitable than private firms and return only 4.9% on assets compared to 13.2% for private companies.5﻿ These companies dominate their industries and include the big three energy companies: PetroChina, Sinopec, and China National Offshore Oil Corporation.6﻿ China developed cities around these factories to attract workers. Made to Order NCOs: The PLA’s Targeted Training NCO Program China’s People’s Liberation Army (PLA) established a formal non-commissioned officer (NCO) corps in the late 1990s, partly to address the problem of its enlisted force being largely comprised of unskilled, poorly educated conscripts not well suited to a rapidly modernizing military. In the over two decades since, the PLA has introduced many initiatives to attract more educated enlisted members, such as focusing on recruiting college students (and saving their place for when the return) and recent graduates and even the direct recruitment of college graduates with certain specialties into the PLA as NCOs. Although these initiatives continue, in recent years the PLA has acknowledged that its NCO corps still lacks higher education and technical skills, and that this problem will become more prominent as increasingly modern weapons and equipment place higher demands on personnel.  While the majority of PLA NCOs still likely begin as two-year “conscripts” (the term used for all two-year enlistees despite some of them joining voluntarily) who apply and are selected to remain on active duty after their initial commitment, an increasing number of those in technical expert positions are Targeted Training NCOs (定向培养士官). These “made to order” NCOs are recruited as they graduate high school into a three-year program that includes 2.5 years of service-designated coursework at a civilian polytechnic/vocational college followed by 6 months of military training before being conferred with NCO rank and benefits, bypassing the junior enlisted ranks altogether. With over 20,000 students recruited into the program in 2020 and likely more in 2021, this program has the potential to inject  significant technical knowledge into the PLA’s NCO corps.     According to PLA Air Force (PLAAF) reporting, Targeted Training NCOs are highly demanded by units for their ability to quickly adapt to military life and become technical experts. I

education, training, doctrine, and test.   In other words, where Space Force’s other two field commands deal largely with systems and equipment and timelines and milestones and mission playbooks, we here at STARCOM deal with talent and people and skill and will and culture.   STARCOM’s main activities:   Basic training for new enlisted Guardians, including instilling a Guardian culture Advanced training like the SPACE FLAG exercise and SKIES series of exercises, which provide live and virtual combat training to both U.S. and Coalition forces Online distributed learning courses based on the spacepower disciplines like orbital mechanics Doctrine documents published like sustainment, personnel, and planning Schriever Wargame to explore critical space issues in depth, investigate military utility of new space systems and evolve air, space, and cyberspace doctrine with a focus on deterrence and military operations in a multi-domain environment with international partners Aggressor text_snippet: 5. China’s Military Space Forces Although it maintains a policy of peace in outer space, Beijing nevertheless is actively developing a diverse set of military forces designed to challenge U.S. superiority in the space domain. This is probably because Beijing has come to the conclusion that “whoever controls space will control the Earth”—a common refrain among Chinese military academics, almost certainly driven by their observations of heavy U.S. reliance on space-based assets for precision-guided munitions and joint combat operations. In a high-profile recognition of the importance of outer space, China’s 2015 defense white paper specifically referred to space as “a commanding height in international and strategic competition” that “will be dealt with.” Thus, China has focused on achieving space supremacy through enhancements to its space-based C4ISR (satellite) architecture, but also by developing counterspace capabilities to deny an adversary’s ability to use outer space against Beijing in a conflict. Recent PLA organizational reforms further underscore Beijing’s prioritization of the space domain. On 31 December 2015, President Xi announced the establishment of the PLA Strategic Support Force (PLASSF) to command China’s military space forces.  This chapter begins by describing what we know about the size and force structure of China’s military space forces, recognizing that recent organizational reforms are not fully fleshed out yet. Next, we detail the PLA’s current space capabilities. Finally, we highlight several considerations China will likely have going forward in the space domain.  Size and Force Structure As noted above, President Xi recently announced the establishment of the PLASSF to command China’s military space forces. Based on available information, we cannot accurately assess the size of the PLASSF because we do not know how many personnel and units are being redirected to it. PLA Force Reductions – Feb 2023  PLA Downsizing History RMHJ, 13 September 2003, p. 3  May 1950 January 1952 August 1953 January 1957 September 1975 March 1980 June 1982 1985 September 1997 September 2003 January 2016   The 13 September 2003 issue of Renmin Haijun has a half-page article outlining the key aspects of the ten times the PLA has implemented a downsizing of its force. The terms used are da caijun (大裁军), which is translated as disarmament, and jingjian (精简), which is best translated as to reduce, retrench, cut, or simplify. Jingjian is usually followed by a percentage. However, the PLAAF’s newspaper, Kongjun Bao, refers to the September 2003 downsizing as organizational structure adjustment (bianzhi tizhi tiaozheng 编制体制调整).  When the PRC was established,  the PLA had 5.5 million troops. The bulk of the force was the Army’s infantry. The Navy, Air Force, and special forces were basically non-existent.   The first downsizing occurred in May 1950, when the CMC decided to reduce the size of the force by one million by downsizing the infantry branch and to begin building up the Navy and Air Force services along with the Army’s artillery, armored, engineering, chemical defense, communications (signals), and railway branches. The move was also intended to help support national economic development. By the end of 1950, the overall size of the force had been reduced by 17.1 percent.   The move to reduce the size of the force halted when the Korean War broke out. In fact, the size of the force expanded, where it reached 6.27 million by October 1951.   In January 1952, the PLA began implementing its second downsizing. This included reducing the number of general departments, headquarters staff members, and infantry units, while expanding the number of people in the new services and schools. The PLA also turned over 950,000 troops to the Ministry of Public Security. This left the PLA with about three million troops.   In August 1953, the PLA began implementing its th

The Central Military Commission is the abbreviation of the Central Military Commission of the 
Communist Party of China and the Central Military Commission of the People's Republic of China. It is 
"the highest military command and military decision-making body of the Communist Party of China". It 
leads the armed forces of the country and is responsible for the highest military decision-making and 
military command of the party and the country. , according to the party's line, principles, policies and 
the needs of national security and development, determine the military strategy, lead the military 
construction, is the highest commanding agency of the People's Liberation Army.

The Central Military Commission of the Communist Party of China was created by the

In October 1925, in order to meet the needs of armed struggle, the second enlarged meeting of the

——The Eighth Great Disarmament From May 23 to June 6, 1985, the enlarged meeting of the Central Military Commission made a major decision to implement a strategic change in the guiding ideology of national defense and army construction, that is, from the state of wartime to the track of peacetime construction. On July 11 of the same year, the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China and the Central Military Commission approved and forwarded the "Army System Reform, Streamlining and Reorganization Plan". According to the requirements, the tasks of reforming the system and streamlining and reorganizing this time are: first, to reduce the total number of military personnel and reduce the total number of troops by 1 million; By 1987, the total number of PLA personnel had been reduced from 4.238 million to 3.235 million. After that, further reductions were made. By 1990, the total number of personnel in the whole army had been reduced to 3.199 million, and a total of 1.039 million personnel had been reduced, accounting for 24.5% of the total number of personnel before the reduction. Through this one-million-dollar disarmament, the People's Liberation Army has taken a big step forward in refining, combining, and improving efficiency. One is to compress the size of the army, reduce the quantity and improve the quality. The second is to withdraw and compress the organization. 11 major military regions were streamlined and merged into 7, and 4 major military regions were merged, reducing support personnel. The third is to set up an army group army and army aviation force, increase the proportion of technical arms, and reduce the number of units above the military level by more than 30 in the whole army. Fourth, some leadership and management systems have been initially rationalized, and the staff of the headquarters of the Central Military Commission has been reduced by nearly half. Understanding the Chief of Staff’s Responsibilities Ken Allen (19 August 2012) This paper tries to explain the role of the chief of staff (参谋长), who is the Director of the Headquarters Department. In the U.S. military, this person would be equivalent to a chief of staff in a headquarters. One way to describe the chief of staff is that he is the senior “primary staff officer,” directly responsible to the commander and political commissar, and is in charge of overseeing the operations of the Headquarters Department and supervising the work of the Logistics and Equipment/Armament Departments, but not the work of the Political Department.   Each chief of staff has several deputy chiefs of staff, of whom one would be the equivalent to the Director of Operations or the “3”, as in the A-3 for Army and Air Force organizations, N-3 for Navy organizations, and the J-3 for a joint command such as PACOM. To begin with, none of the PLA’s encyclopedias or dictionaries, including the 1997 PLA Military Terminology Dictionary, has an entry for chief of staff. Therefore, I searched through our available books that dealt with command (指挥) and found the following information. There is also information about other key terms that deal with the chief of staff, some of which comes from previous interviews with PLA officers. Sources The information comes from the following books: Jiang Daohong, Liu Leibo, and Liu Huimin, eds., Discussion on Chief of Staff Quality (参谋长素质论), (Beijing: National Defense University Press, January 2007). Yuan Wenxian, ed., Discussion of Headquarters Department Building (司令部建设论), (Beijing: National Defense University Press, July 2003). Chapter 7 (pages 146-173) provides an overview of the role of the chief staff, including the history of the position. Zhang Yuliang, Science of Campaigns (战役学), (Beijing: NDU Press, May 2006). text_snippet: Assessing China’s Military Academic Institution Structure (Ken Allen: 6 September 2011) Introduction This paper provides an introduction to the organizational structure of China’s military academic institutions (军事院校), as well as methods for finding and analyzing information about them. Although military academic institutions include the People’s Liberation Army (PLA / 解放军) and the People’s Armed Police (PAP / 人民武装警察部队), the paper focuses on the PLA. This paper is NOT intended to be all encompassing or to provide profiles on individual institutions. The information does include the following: Types of military academic institutions Comprehensive universities  Unit and major/specialty codes  Official names and websites  Protocol order  Organizational grade structure and military unit cover designators (MUCDs) Degrees  Education and training terms and concepts  Class year identification  Leadership  Party committees and Party membership  Administrative and functional departments  Academic departments and specialties Organization names and number designators  Laboratories and centers  PLA Air Force (PLAAF) flight colleges  Student organizational structure  Funding codes  Journals. The paper also discusses inconsistencies in PLA translations of the names of the institutions and their subordinate organizations into English.  Although this paper states the PLA has 67 officer academic institutions, some institutions are apparently not listed. In addition, the PLA instituted a new set of reforms in August 2011 that is in the process of altering that number as well as the name for several of the institutions. For example, the Air Force Radar Academy (空军雷达学院) is to be renamed Air Force Early Warning College (空军预警学院), and the Artillery Academy is to be renamed the Army Officer Academy (陆军军官学院). CMC History - Wikipedia - October 2022 https://zh.m.wikipedia.org/zh-hans/中央军事委员会  中央军事委员会   此条目介绍的是中国共产党和中华人民共和国的最高军事领导机关。关于其他用法，请见“中央军事委员会 (消歧义)”。 中国共产党和中华人民共和国中央军事委员会[1]，通称中央军事委员会，简称中央军委、军委，是中国共产党和中华人民共和国武装力量最高军事领导机构的总称[2]，包括以下两个机构： 中国共产党中央军事委员会 中华人民共和国中央军事委员会 1983年，中华人民共和国中央军事委员会成立后，中共中央军事委员会和国家中央军事委员会的组成人员基本相同，实际上是“一个机构两块牌子”。中共中央军事委员会由中国共产党中央委员会产生或罢免。国家中央军事委员会由全国人民代表大会产生或罢免，对全国人大及其常务委员会负责。对国家武装力量发布命令、授予军衔警衔、授予勋章奖章时一般以“中华人民共和国中央军事委员会”名义发布[3]。 历史编辑 建国前编辑 1925年9月，鉴于中共从事军队工作的党员日趋增多，莫斯科东方大学归国学生王一飞向中共中央总书记陈独秀建议成立中央军事委员会，得到采纳[4]。1925年9月28日至10月2日在北京召开的中共第四届中央执行委员会第二次扩大会议决定，成立中央军事部门即军事运动委员会。1925年12月，中共中央在上海召开了着重研究军事工作的十二月会议，会议修改后的《军事工作决议》（草案）第五条指出，“党中央下设军事部，军事部下设特派员，军事部内设组织和情报两个部门”。1925年12月12日中共中央发布通告，将军事运动委员会改为中央军事部，主要从事军事组织联络和情报工作。军事部由张国焘、王一飞和任弼时三位中央委员组成，张国焘兼任主任，任弼时长期主持团中央工作，王一飞成为实际负责人；颜昌颐、奚佐尧先后任技术书记。1926年11月上旬为准备上海工人武装起义，中共中央决定将中央军事部改为中央军事委员会兼上海区军事委员会，周恩来任书记。1926年11月16日上海区委单独成立军事委员会，中央军委仍然存在。四一二事件后，中央军委随中央机关于1927年5月下旬从上海迁到武汉[5]，5月25日召开的中共中央政治局常委会将中央军事委员会改称中央军人部（仍习惯性称中央军事部），决定由周恩来任中央军事部部长，并主持成立特务工作处，其中情报股负责人为董醒吾（国民政府武汉侦缉大队队长）。7月下旬部长周恩来前往南昌领导武装起义，军事部由王一飞负责[5]。 1927年9月底10月初，中央机关从武汉迁回上海后，军委人员也陆续到达上海。11月14日，在上海召开的中共临时中央政治局常委会议决定调整中央组织机构，不设部，而在中央临时政治局常委下设立组织局，由罗亦农、周恩来、李维汉三人组成，领导文书、交通、会计、组织、宣传、调查出版、分配、军事、特务等科和妇女运动委员会。周恩来分管军事和特务工作，其中军事科科长是李鸣珂，实际由周恩来负责。1928年6月中共六大会议上的《军事工作决议案（草案）》规定：“中国共产党的一切军事工作都应集中于中国共产党中央军事部。各地应设立军事委员会，受地方党部之一般指导而工作，但于军事技术方面，则受中央军事部之指挥。”1928年7月恢复“中央军事部”，杨殷（1928年7月20日至1929年8月24日）、周恩来先后任部长。1928年10月25日，中共中央颁布的《各级党部军事机关之组织大纲》，重申中央军事部设立军事委员会，作为就军事问题进行讨论和建议的机关。1930年2月中共中央政治局会议决定将中央军事部与军委合二为一，直属中央政治局，中央军委委员 9人，周恩来、关向应、曾中生为常委。1931年1月15日，苏区中央局成立，同时成立（苏区）中央革命军事委员会，1931年6月改称中华苏维埃共和国中央革命军事委员会（简称“苏区中革军委”），负责指挥红军作战，朱德任主席。在上海的中央军事委员会不再直接指挥红军作战，而“主要负责国民党统治区的兵运、情报等军事工作及对苏区中革军委、各主力红军重大决策的指导”。1931年1月30日，中共中央政治局临时会议决定，中央军委委员为：周恩来、陈郁、温裕成、王云程、艾逸之、聂荣臻、陈赓；陈赓兼任谍报科科长，曾希圣、刘仲华等为主要工作人员。1931年9月22日，中共临时中央政治局（简称临时中央）成立，博古负总责，临时中央领导下的中央军事委员会“主要负责指导白区军事斗争，开展情报、联络工作，进行对国民党军的兵运工作，向苏区派遣干部和提供药品、器材等”。原中央军委书记周恩来、代理书记李富春以及军委参谋长聂荣臻相继于1931年12月从上海去中央苏区，陈赓赴鄂豫皖苏区。上海的中央军事委员会增补李超时、武胡景、洪易、柯庆施为委员，武胡景成为军委负

text_snippet: An Overview of PLA Officer Grades and Ranks Ken Allen The following information comes from the PLA Officer Rank Regulations (zhongguo renmin jiefangjun junguan junxian tiaoli; 中国人民解放军军官军衔条例) that was amended on 20 December 2002 and the PLA Active-Duty Officer Law (zhonghua renmin gongheguo xianyi junguan fa; 中华人民共和国现役军官法) that was published on 28 December 2000 (See Appendices A and B).   According to the Chinese Military Encyclopedia, the first ranks Regulations were passed on 1 July 1988 and amended on 12 May 1994.  The Regulations were amended again on 1 July 1998. The Regulations were last amended on 20 December 2002. The 1988 Regulations re-established officer ranks after they were abolished in 1965. Of note, the National People’s Congress (NPC) passed a Military Service Law in May 1984 that laid the ground work for reinstituting ranks, but the follow-on PLA Officer Rank Regulations were not implemented until 1988. The 1984 Law was amended on 29 December 1998 (See Appendix C).  The 1988 Regulations authorized three ranks for all but the CMC chairman, vice-chairmen, and members grades. The 1994 Regulations reduced the number of ranks per grade to two.   According to the Chinese Military Encyclopedia (Supplement 1) the first Active-Duty Officer Law was passed on 28 December 2000. Although it was based on the above mentioned officer rank Regulations, the Law addresses grades only and does not address ranks.   The information below comes from the 2000 amended Law and the 1998 Regulations. Neither the Law nor the Regulations had figures, so I developed the figures to provide a visual perspective of the information. The PLA has five officer career tracks as follows: Military officer (junshi junguan; 军事军官) [Note: They are also called command officers (zhihui junguan; 指挥军官)] Political officer (zhengzhi junguan; 政治军官) Logistics officer (houqin junguan; 后勤军官) Armament officer (zhuangbei junguan; 装备军官) [Note: The real translation is equipment officer, but armament is used

Overview of the PRC’s Central Military Commission As the PRC prepares for its 20th Chinese Communist Party Congress in late 2022, I have put together some history of the Central Military Commission (CMC) to speculate about what the new CMC will most likely look like. It begins by looking at the 2002, 2007, 2012, and 2017 CMCs and then makes some speculation about the 2022 CMC. CMC History As shown in the Appendix, the CMC has gone through multiple changes since 1949. It is my opinion that the CMC has often changed in order to allow certain people to become the vice chairmen or members. For example, in 2012, the two personnel who were qualified to become the vice chairmen were the Navy commander, Wu Shengli, and the Air Force commander, Xu Qiliang; however, it is my opinion that several things happened to allow Fan Changlong to become the “senior” vice chairman. First, I believe  that the Army-dominated PLA was not about to allow not having an Army officer as a vice chairman, so they selected Fan Changlong, who did not meet the time in grade criteria to become a vice chairman, so he “skipped a grade” to take the billet. Second, since it then boiled down to Wu Shengli and Xu Qiliang for the second billet, I believe that Wu decided he could make more of an impact by remaining as the Navy commander. In addition, had Xu Qiliang remained as the Air Force commander, then Ma Xiaotian would have met his mandatory retirement age before  the next Party congress, so he would not have been able to become  the commander.  According to Alice Miller’s chapter in The PLA as Org 2.0, retirement norms for CMC members appear to have consolidated during Hu Jintao’s leadership of the CMC.  Under the 1994 revision of the 1988 “Active Service Regulations,” specific retirement ages were set down for PLA officers up through military region grade leaders, and  regular members of the CMC were expected to retire at 70, though with the possibility under some circumstances at 72, and there was no

Key Terms and Concepts (Ken Allen, 9 September 2018) One of the most difficult aspects of any study on the PLA is deciding on the best method to translate key PLA terms and concepts so that they make sense in a U.S. military context. Unfortunately, most Chinese military dictionaries provide only a direct English translation for key terms. They do not provide detailed explanations or definitions for those terms. Sometimes, PLA terms cannot be easily translated into English, or the common English translation means something different for the U.S. military than it does for the PLA. For example, a PLA Air Force or Naval Aviation zhongdui, which is normally translated as a squadron, has only two to four aircraft, while a U.S. Air Force squadron has 15-24 aircraft.   Therefore, some PLA terms are best translated into English, while other terms are best left in Chinese. For example, it is sometimes best to use the following Chinese terms: zongdui, zhidui, dadui, zhongdui, and fendui, rather than assigning them an English translation. These terms are discussed below.   The following sub-sections list key PLA terms and explain their meaning based on my analysis of how the PLA uses the term in its newspapers, journals, and books. In some cases, Project Asia has discussed these terms during interviews with various PLA officials. The terms are organized into the following four categories:  CMC, Services, Branches, and Units PLA Leadership and Administrative Organization Structure Academies and Schools CMC, Services, Branches, and Units Zhongyang junwei (中央军委): Zhongyang junwei refers to the Military Commission (junshi weiyuanhui; 军事委员会) of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). I use Central Military Commission and the acronym CMC.  Junzhong (军种) and (bingzhong; 兵种): The PLA has three services (junzhong: 军种): the army or ground forces (lujun; 陆军), navy (haijun; 海军), and air force (kongjun; 空军), which are almost always listed in protocol order based their da

PLA Officer Subsidies Ken Allen, 7 April 2013 Source: PLA Officer Handbook (中国人民解放军官手册) PLA officer billet Subsidies/Stipends (岗位津贴) are divided into 10 categories as shown below: Grassroots officers (基层军官岗位津贴) per month Company leader grade: 400 RMB Company deputy leader grade: 300 RMB Platoon grade: 200 RMB Emergency mobilization combat units (应急机动作战部队岗位津贴) per month 100 RMB Course subsidies (课时补助) for academic Institution Instructors (院校教员) as well as leadership billets in teaching and research labs (教研室) and student teams (学员队) per course Instructors: 30 RMB Teaching and research lab leaders: 400 RMB Teaching and research lab deputy leaders: 300 RMB Student team leaders: 300 RMB Student team deputy leaders: 200 RMB Brigade and regiment grade organization leaders (旅团级单位领导职务军官岗位津贴) subsidy per month Leaders: 500 RMB Deputy leaders and administrative and functional department leaders: 300 RMB Aviation specialty billet subsidy (航天专业岗位津贴), which includes aircrew, aircraft maintenance, and airborne paratroop billets Based on their flight grade (grade 1 to special grade), pilots subsidies are divided into 15 steps (档次) that range from 600 to 3,600 RMB per month Non-pilot aircrew officers (navigation, engineers, communications, and fire control) are divided into 6 steps that range from 700 to 1,600 RMB per month Mission subsidies (任务津贴): Depending on the aircraft type and model and the billet, aircrew members receive 150 to 400 RMB per flight hour, instructor aircrew members receive 180 to 360 RMB per hour Stop flying (e.g., grounded) subsidies (停飞津贴) Fighter pilots with at least 15 years and other aircraft 17 years: Depends on  their aircraft and years of flying that can average 50% and a one-time payment up to 200% Maintenance specialty billets (机务专业岗位津贴): There are 8 specialty grades (等级) that range from 550 to 1,600 RMB per month Airborne paratroop specialty billets (跳伞专业岗位津贴): There are 8 specialty grades that range from 200 to 1,200 RMB per month. filename: TDATB Feedback from LtCol Kaleb Harkema.docx
text_snippet: TDATB Feedback from LtCol Kaleb Harkema 30 Dec 2021 All, Forwarding some work done by Key Allen and Brendan Mulvaney that discusses what happens the day after a Taiwan Unification conflict is over.  The work includes the attached three items: 1) a long draft report on "The Day After The Battle" that looks at what happens if Taiwan wins or loses a conflict; 2) an article published by Air University that only looks at the "win" component, and 3) a briefing  that lays out each possibility. I think it is a topic we should be thinking about, from the tactical all the way to strategic level.  Below are a few comments from my end after a first read of the products, Most importantly, I think it is important to start thinking through this in depth.  That being said, I think there is a the need to expand the thinking on it.  I think framing this as "Taiwan wins" or "Taiwan loses" falls short of the importance of the discussion.  In addition to those questions, this type of work would need different lenses looking at modified questions such as - What if PRC wins or PRC loses, and What if the U.S. wins or U.S. loses. Some of those considerations are covered briefly, but I think it is important enough to add more on that aspect of the discussion. While this is a great start/topic, I feel that the conclusions somewhat miss the mark.  In the longer word doc they state that the economic impact "is one of the most difficult, yet one of the most important, questions."   The Conclusion portion of the longer paper then states that "One of the most important conclusion is that, "win" or "lose", Taiwan will be affected the most." with further discussion about U.S. decisions on diplomatic recognition, military garrison issues, PLA post-conflict buildup, and the potential emergence of a new "Cold War."    What is not covered is the blow or boon to the U.S. standing in the Asia Pacific and Globally if "Taiwan wins" or "Taiwan loses", and the associated blow or boon to PRC regionally/globall

Appendix A: Taiwan’s Economic Situation According to a report by the U.S. Council on Foreign Relations in January 2020, “Taiwan began investing in China after reform policies were implemented by PRC leader Deng Xiaoping in the late 1970s. Despite intermittent friction, the cross-strait economic relationship has blossomed. China entered the World Trade Organization in 2001 and, within a month, Taiwan entered as ‘Chinese Taipei.’ The island holds member, observer, or other status in more than fifty organizations, such as the Asian Development Bank (ADB), Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) committees, and regional fishery organizations. China is Taiwan’s largest trading partner, accounting for nearly 30% of the island’s total trade, and trade between the two reached $150.5 billion in 2018 (up from $35 billion in 1999). China and Taiwan have also agreed to allow banks, insurers, and other financial service providers to work in both markets. In 2015, the number of direct flights between them hit just under 900 per week, up from 270 in 2009. Nevertheless, the economic relationship has experienced some hiccups in recent years. Taiwan investment in the mainland declined for its fourth consecutive year in 2018, and mainland investment in Taiwan has increased at a slower rate than before.” Today, Taiwan is one of the biggest investors in China. Between 1991 and the end of February 2019, approved investment in China comprised 43,401 cases totaling US$183.4 billion. In 2018, the value of cross-strait trade was US$150.5 billion. Also shown is each import country’s percentage of total Taiwan exports.
