PLA Military Diplomacy During 2023 Kenneth W. Allen Introduction As the People’s Republic of China (PRC) enters 2024, it is important to know how the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) conducted its military diplomacy during 2023 in order to support the Chinese Communist Party (CCP aka CPC). Therefore, this report provides information concerning the PLA’s military diplomacy during 2023 based about 250 articles published in two of the PRC’s Ministry of National Defense (MND) English-language websites and 25 articles in MND’s Chinese-language website. Of note, 23 of the 25 Chinese-language articles were identical to the English-language articles. Appendix A provides a list of previous articles/reports on military diplomacy since 2000 that lays the foundation for this report. The report is organized into the following 12 Sections and 2 Appendices:  Section 1: Key Developments during 2023 Section 2: Senior-level Hosted Visits and Visits Abroad Section 3: PLA Navy Military Diplomacy Activities Section 4: Coast Guard Military Diplomacy Activities Section 5: PLA Army Military Diplomacy Activities Section 6: PLA Air Force Military Diplomacy Activities Section 7: High-level Meetings at Home and Abroad Section 8: United Nations Peacekeeping Operations Section 9: Humanitarian and Medical-related Activities Section 10: PLA Academies and Cadet Military Diplomacy Activities Section 11: Border Defense Military Diplomacy Activities Section 12: Foreign Attache Visits to PLA Facilities Appendix A: Previous Military Diplomacy Reports Appendix B: Acronyms Data Explanation It is important to note that all of the information in each section is taken directly from the articles in the MND website or from Wikipedia and the relevant citations have been included. In some cases, the information is condensed, so it is not exactly word for word. I did not provide any analysis. The analysis of the information will be provided in the CMSI China Maritime Report. While China almost froze its military diplomacy with the United States in 2023, the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) was busy enhancing its cooperation with other foreign militaries. This report will review China’s military diplomacy activities in 2023, including senior-level visits and meetings, joint exercises, naval port calls, and humanitarian activities. Key findings are listed below:  The report reveals that the PLA has been actively involved in military diplomacy, with 66 defense exchanges, 24 joint military exercises, and 27 naval port calls recorded in 2023. Showing the Red Flag: The PLA Navy  as an Instrument of China’s Foreign Policy Kenneth W. Allen   A man-of-war is the best ambassador. Oliver Cromwell, 1600s  As the Navy plays a role in all historical periods, whether peace or war, it is also a means of pursuing national foreign policy. Navies possess many specific characteristics that differ from those of the other armed forces. The Navy has international capabilities of free navigation on the high seas, and in peacetime it can cruise the world’s seas, even conducting limited operations outside the territorial waters of hostile countries...but even more important is its peacetime, national foreign policy role.  Vice Admiral Chen Mingshan, PLA Navy Deputy Commander, 1993  Introduction  When United States security policymakers and experts think of military engagement, they tend to focus on how the U.S. interacts with foreign militaries. Often overlooked is the fact that what the U.S. does is not unique, and that foreign militaries also have similar engagement policies. One of the most visible forms of military diplomacy that countries around the world engage in is high-level naval exchanges and port calls.  Over the past decade, most Asian nations have taken advantage of naval diplomacy to further their foreign policy goals, but no country’s activities can come close to the scope of what the United States Navy does in the region. The region’s naval activities have increasingly grown from bilateral to multilateral. The 17-member Western Pacific Naval Symposium (WPNS) has held eight biennial meetings and multiple preparatory workshops since it was established in 1988. Since 1995, Indonesia, South Korea, India, and Japan have hosted International Fleet Reviews, involving naval vessels from as many as twenty-one countries. In accordance with the mutual agreements reached in 1992 by their ministries of national defense, the PRC and ROK have conducted military-to-military contacts in three major areas: high-level visits, functional exchanges, and military research cooperation. PRC-ROK Military Relations In accordance with the mutual agreements reached in 1992 by their ministries of national defense, the Republic of Korea (ROK) and China have conducted military-to-military contacts in three major areas: high-level visits, functional exchanges, and military research cooperation. Security cooperation in all three areas shows a limited, yet improving, relationship. When Presidents Roh Tae Woo and Kim Young Sam visited China in September 1992 and March 1994, respectively, they were accompanied by their Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Following these visits, a few other ROK generals in charge of policy and defense intelligence began visits to China in 1994. Official visits from the ROK’s Ministry of National Defense (MND) began in November 1997, when Vice Defense Minister Lee Jung-rin visited Beijing. This visit was followed up in August 1999, when Defense Minister Cho Seong-tae became the highest ranking official to visit China. As a result of his visit, China and the ROK established regular military relations, which means that there will be reciprocal visits on a two-year cycle by at least one high-level military official, plus one to two functional level visits.   It was not until December 1996 that China began sending high-level military delegations to Seoul, when Major General Luo Bin, the PLA’s Director of the Foreign Affairs Office, became the first active-duty PLA general to visit Seoul since the Korean War. The first PLAAF commander to visit South Korea was Liu Shunyao in 2001, and his counterpart paid the first visit by a ROK Air Force chief of staff to China in 2005. text_snippet: PLA Diplomacy in Asia: Content and Consequences Kenneth Allen  China has established military relations with more than 150 countries. It has established over 100 military attaché offices abroad, and 85 countries have set up military attaché offices in China. In recent years, China has sent over 1,000 military students to more than 20 countries, and 19 military academies in China have established exchange relations with their counterparts in 25 countries. During 2003 and 2004, more than 1,245 military personnel from 91 countries studied in Chinese military academies.  China’s National Defense: 2004  China’s military, the People’s Liberation Army (PLA), is an integral part of the People’s Republic of China’s (PRC’s) foreign policy program. Each major military component, to include the Central Military Commission (CMC), four General Departments, Army, Navy (PLAN), Air Force (PLAAF), and Second Artillery, fits into the PRC’s, PLA’s, and each individual services’ foreign relations program.  The purpose of this paper is to address the PLA’s foreign relations program in Asia, but this cannot be accomplished without also looking at the PLA’s worldwide activities. Nor can this be done without examining how the PLA’s foreign relations fit into China’s overall diplomacy program in the Asia-Pacific region.  This paper begins by providing an historical context for China’s foreign military relations program and briefly discusses primary sources of information on this program. It then examines how the white papers describe the role of international security cooperation in China’s overall foreign relations and national defense strategy. The article also looks at the various types of international security cooperation that took place during the 10th Five-Year Plan. The paper concludes by looking at three case studies for China’s foreign relations with three Asia-Pacific countries: Japan, India, and Australia. text_snippet: China’s Air Force Foreign Relations Program and Implications for Interaction with the U.S. Air Force By Major Category: China Brief, Military/Security, Foreign Policy, China and the Asia-Pacific Kenneth Allen, U.S. Air Force (Retired)  The People’s Liberation Army’s (PLA) foreign relations program is an integral component of the Chinese government’s comprehensive foreign relations effort, which is guided by national policy and moderated by the state of relations with each individual country. The PLA’s overall foreign relations program is managed by the Ministry of National Defense (MND) Foreign Affairs Office (FAO), which, in turn, is a front for the General Staff Department’s (GSD) FAO and the Central Military Commission’s (CMC) General Office. PLAAF Headquarters’ Foreign Affairs Bureau (FAB) manages the PLAAF program under MND FAO’s guidance. As part of China’s overall program, the PLAAF has gradually expanded from merely exchanging delegations to conducting combined exercises with the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) and individual countries. In 2001, the PLAAF Command College created a program for foreign officers that, since 2009, includes PLAAF officers and pilots. In addition, the PLAAF has begun to perform military operations other than war (MOOTW) abroad to support national goals. This article first addresses how the PLAAF interacts with the international community. It concludes with a focus on relations with the U.S. Air Force (USAF).  PLAAF Interaction with the International Community The PLAAF interacts with the international community and foreign militaries through a number of channels. In November 2014, the PLAAF reported that more than 50 foreign air force commanders (chiefs of staff) have visited China in the past few years, and that it has sent more than 600 senior science and technology officers from units and academic institutions to visit and study abroad. text_snippet: The Top Trends in China’s Military Diplomacy China Brief, 1 May 2015 By Kenneth Allen Global outreach is the inevitable course for ’s forces to deal with various threats and to fulfill multiple tasks in the process of realizing the “Chinese Dream” and a strong military. ’s armed forces accelerated their global interactions in 2013. Chinese forces have appeared in many corners of the world, including North America and Oceana, the Pacific and the .  has deployed more than 10,000 personnel—an unprecedented number—to participate in combined drills, international peacekeeping, naval escorts, humanitarian aid and other non-war operations. Media from home and abroad have commented that 2013 was the Chinese military’s most active year ever in the world arena. China’s forces went further and more frequently into the world and engaged in international affairs more thoroughly, demonstrating their increasing maturity and confidence. China wants to show the world its tremendous achievements in national defense modernization, its sincere wish to develop friendship and deepen cooperation with forces around the world, and its determination to maintain its national interests and world peace. — Armed Forces [1] Introduction This article identifies the top trends and events concerning ’s People’s Liberation Army’s (PLA) military diplomacy. The PLA has clearly expanded its involvement in international humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HA/DR) and military operations other than war (MOOTW) activities, such as the search for the missing Malaysian airliner (MH370) and deployment of the Peace Ark hospital ship, which are linked to Chinese President Xi Jinping’s “China Dream” concept that includes making China the “world’s dominant power” and “a stronger nation with a strong military.” As such, the PLA Navy (PLAN) is spending longer and more complex deployments at sea, including port calls by the antipiracy escort task forces and other task forces around the world, while the Army,

text_snippet: Assessing the Growing PLA Air Force Foreign Relations Program Publication: China Brief Volume: 12 Issue: 9 April 26, 2012 Category: China Brief, Military/Security, Foreign Policy, China and the Asia-Pacific  By: Kenneth Allen, Emma Kelly  PLAAF Commander Xu Qiliang with Foreign Military Students in China The People’s Liberation Army Air Force’s (PLAAF) foreign relations program is an increasingly important component of the PLA’s overall foreign relations program. As part of China’s overall program, it gradually has expanded from merely exchanging delegations to conducting combined exercises with individual countries and the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO). In 2001, the PLAAF Command College created a program for foreign officers that, since 2009, includes PLAAF officers. In addition, the PLAAF has begun to perform military operations other than war (MOOTW) abroad to support national goals. This article addresses how the PLA interacts with the international community and then discusses the ways in which the PLAAF implements its foreign relations program with a focus at the end on PLAAF relations with the U.S. Air Force (USAF). How the PLA Interacts with the International Community The PLA interacts with the international community and foreign militaries through a number of channels. Actual PLA military diplomacy includes, but is not limited to, the following activities: Strategic security dialogues; The exchange of military attaché offices and the establishment of embassy/consulate websites; The establishment of a Ministry of National Defense Information Office and spokesman system; High-level military exchanges; Functional and educational military exchanges; PLA Navy port calls; Combined exercises with foreign militaries; The opening of military exercises and operational units to foreign observers; Peace-keeping, anti-piracy and MOOTW; Humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HA/DR) [1]. Headquarters Pacific Air Forces, and Washington, D.C.  He has written extensively on China’s Air Force, including “China’s
Air Force Enters the 21st Century” (a 1995 RAND study for the Pentagon’s Project Air Force), “People’s Republic of China’s
People’s Liberation Army Air Force: An Organizational History,” “Reform of China’s Air Force,” “PLAAF Modernization: An
Assessment,” “PLA Air Force Logistics and Maintenance: What has Changed?”, and “PLAAF Operations and Modernization.”
He received a BA from the University of California at Davis, a BA from the University of Maryland in Asian Studies, and an
MA from Boston University in International Relations.
Eric A. McVadon
Rear Admiral McVadon was the US Defense and Naval Attaché at the American Embassy in Beijing from July 1990 - July
1992.  He retired from the Navy in 1992 and is consulting, writing, and speaking in North America, Europe, and the Far East
on Asia-Pacific security matters, Chinese military and proliferation issues, Taiwan defense concerns, and security developments
involving China, Japan and the Korean Penins

PLA Military Strategy White Paper for 2015 http://military.people.com.cn/n/2015/0526/c52936-27057959.html  中国的军事战略（全文） 2015年05月26日10:54     分享到... 分享到人人  分享到QQ空间  　　中国的军事战略 　　（２０１５年５月） 　　中华人民共和国国务院新闻办公室 　　 目　录 　　 前　言 　　 一、国家安全形势 　　 二、军队使命和战略任务 　　 三、积极防御战略方针 　　 四、军事力量建设发展 　　 五、军事斗争准备 　　 六、军事安全合作 　　 前言 　　 当今世界正面临前所未有之大变局，当代中国正处于改革发展的关键阶段。中国人民在为实现中华民族伟大复兴中国梦的奋斗中，希望同世界各国一道共护和平、共谋发展、共享繁荣。 　　 中国同世界的命运紧密相连、息息相关，世界繁荣稳定是中国的机遇，中国和平发展也是世界的机遇。中国将始终不渝走和平发展道路，奉行独立自主的和平外交政策和防御性国防政策，反对各种形式的霸权主义和强权政治，永远不称霸，永远不搞扩张。中国军队始终是维护世界和平的坚定力量。 　　 建设巩固国防和强大军队是中国现代化建设的战略任务，是国家和平发展的安全保障。军事战略是筹划和指导军事力量建设和运用的总方略，服从服务于国家战略目标。站在新的历史起点上，中国军队适应国家安全环境新变化，紧紧围绕实现中国共产党在新形势下的强军目标，贯彻新形势下积极防御军事战略方针，加快推进国防和军队现代化，坚决维护国家主权、安全、发展利益，为实现“两个一百年”奋斗目标和中华民族伟大复兴的中国梦提供坚强保障。 　　 一、国家安全形势 　　 当今时代，世界多极化、经济全球化、社会信息化深入发展，国际社会日益成为你中有我、我中有你的命运共同体，和平、发展、合作、共赢成为不可阻挡的时代潮流。 　　 国际形势深刻演变，国际力量对比、全球治理体系结构、亚太地缘战略格局和国际经济、科技、军事竞争格局正在发生历史性变化。维护和平的力量上升，制约战争的因素增多，在可预见的未来，世界大战打不起来，总体和平态势可望保持。但是，霸权主义、强权政治和新干涉主义将有新的发展，各种国际力量围绕权力和权益再分配的斗争趋于激烈，恐怖主义活动日益活跃，民族宗教矛盾、边界领土争端等热点复杂多变，小战不断、冲突不止、危机频发仍是一些地区的常态，世界依然面临现实和潜在的局部战争威胁。 　　 中国发展仍将处于可以大有作为的重要战略机遇期，外部环境总体有利。国家综合国力、核心竞争力、抵御风险能力显著增强，国际地位和国际影响力显著提高，人民生活显著改善，社会大局保持稳定。作为一个发展中大国，中国仍然面临多元复杂的安全威胁，遇到的外部阻力和挑战逐步增多，生存安全问题和发展安全问题、传统安全威胁和非传统安全威胁相互交织，维护国家统一、维护领土完整、维护发展利益的任务艰巨繁重。 　　 随着世界经济和战略重心加速向亚太地区转移，美国持续推进亚太“再平衡”战略，强化其地区军事存在和军事同盟体系。日本积极谋求摆脱战后体制，大幅调整军事安全政策，国家发展走向引起地区国家高度关注。个别海上邻国在涉及中国领土主权和海洋权益问题上采取挑衅性举动，在非法“占据”的中方岛礁上加强军事存在。一些域外国家也极力插手南海事务，个别国家对华保持高频度海空抵近侦察，海上方向维权斗争将长期存在。一些陆地领土争端也依然存在。朝鲜半岛和东北亚地区局势存在诸多不稳定和不确定因素。地区恐怖主义、分裂主义、极端主义活动猖獗，也对中国周边安全稳定带来不利影响。 　　 台湾问题事关国家统一和长远发展，国家统一是中华民族走向伟大复兴的历史必然。近年来两岸关系保持和平发展良好势头，但影响台海局势稳定的根源并未消除，“台独”分裂势力及其分裂活动仍然是两岸关系和平发展的最大威胁。维护国家政治安全和社会稳定的任务艰巨繁重，“东突”“藏独”分裂势力危害严重，特别是“东突”暴力恐怖活动威胁升级，反华势力图谋制造“颜色革命”，国家安全和社会稳定面临更多挑战。随着国家利益不断拓展，国际和地区局势动荡、恐怖主义、海盗活动、重大自然灾害和疾病疫情等都可能对国家安全构成威胁，海外能源资源、战略通道安全以及海外机构、人员和资产安全等海外利益安全问题凸显。 　　 世界新军事革命深入发展，武器装备远程精确化、智能化、隐身化、无人化趋势明显，太空和网络空间成为各方战略竞争新的制高点，战争形态加速向信息化战争演变。世界主要国家积极调整国家安全战略和防务政策，加紧推进军事转型，重塑军事力量体系。军事技术和战争形态的革命性变化，对国际政治军事格局产生重大影响，对中国军事安全带来新的严峻挑战。 　　 二、军队使命和战略任务 　　 中国的

text_snippet: Dave Finkelstein on 2013 Science of Military Strategy 21 March 2014 Here is a volume that is destined to be used judiciously by some but abused, misquoted, and taken out of context by others. For good or ill, it will become the common wisdom just as "Unrestricted Warfare" was. I have already heard respected (suo weide)  PLA experts refer to it as "China's Military Strategy" which it is not. When i get back to my office I will post my comments about the volume from last weekend's PLA conference.  Sorry for taking so long to get back to this. My day job gets in the way of important matters. :-)  I will spare everyone having to read the formal comments about SMS-13 I made at the NBR PLA conference. Below is the Gist. 1. What SMS-2013 is NOT. It is not the PLA's official strategy and does not represent an official institutional view of "The PLA". 2. What it does represent is the institutional view of the Military Strategy Research Department. We can state that with confidence because this department is listed as the "author" of this book. This is important because this is the organization at AMS charged to develop strategic level military theory. So the contents represent this organization's best advice. 3. For those who have a chance to do so, the Forward and Afterward of the book are very helpful in explaining the processes by which the volume was compiled. The 35 officers who comprised the writing committee are listed as well. At the conference, I pointed out that the AMS, like the rest of the PLA, is undergoing generational change, and that some of these officers are new faces rising up. I have chatted about this book with some of them, and they are very conscious of the fact that foreigners will be reading the book. Either the Forward or the Afterward also acknowledges that foreigners will  likelyread it. 4. I am still plowing through the volume and re-reading some important sections, such as the one that discusses future wars the PLA may have to fight. At the end

Chapter 2 Doctrinal Concepts and Guidance Doctrine: The fundamental principles by which the military forces or elements thereof guide their actions in support of national objectives. It is authoritative but requires judgment in application. — JP 1-02  The PLA Air Force has stepped up the development of military theories with strategic theories at the core, and introduced a strategic concept that the Air Force should be capable of offensive and defensive operations. As a result, the Air Force has begun its transition from territorial air defense to simultaneous offensive and defensive operations. — China’s National Defense in 2008  Outside observers trying to understand PLA “doctrine” face a number of challenges. Perhaps the most significant challenge is that an equivalent of the U.S. military term “doctrine” does not even exist in the PLA lexicon. For example, the PLAAF often translates USAF Doctrine Documents as Air Force Regulations (空军条令). Nevertheless, the PLAAF does have operational guidance, terminology, and concepts that U.S. military personnel would refer to collectively as “doctrine.”   The PLAAF’s overall doctrine can be found in writings on what it calls science (科学), thought (思想), and theory (理论). In these writings, the concept of Air Force Military Science (空军军事科学) represents the highest level of doctrine.  The PLAAF has faced a number of challenges in developing its doctrine, including its historical subservience to the Army, lack of combat experience since the 1950s, and lack of the proper weapons and equipment to conduct certain missions.  This chapter briefly discusses the development and content of the PLAAF’s doctrine and operational guidance. Army Domination Since the founding of the PLAAF, its doctrine and operations have been geared to supporting the Army. Roger Cliff PLA Doctrine Issues E-mail exchanges on 29 June 2015    On Mon, Jun 29, 2015 at 11:10 PM, Allen, Ken <ken.allen@defensegp.com> wrote: Roger,    As we have discussed before, you say the PLA has a term for doctrine vs theory (理论). Could you tell me what the term is and if there is an official PLA dictionary  that translates it as doctrine.   Thanks.   Ken   Hi Ken, The term that the PLA appears to use for doctrine is 作战条令.  This is the term that is used in several articles I have found that are clearly about doctrine (encompassing both 战役纲要 and 战斗条令).  Including:

任向东, 〈中国陆海空三军实施新一代作战条令〉, 《了望》, July 23, 1999.

杨志远, 〈我军新一代作战条令出台的前前后后〉, 《解放军报》, October 8, 2008, p. 27.

杨志远, 〈我军编修作战条令的创新发展及启示〉, 《中国军事科学》, No. 6, 2009, pp. 113–118. I am not aware of any official PLA dictionary that translates 作战条令 as "doctrine" (or vice versa) but I haven't really looked. OMTE on Baidu and Baike http://baike.baidu.com/link?url=ak9vKVWXJdhG4tLsT0JyVQ6d6vgvrJKZ9Fl8f80Ufu0nc5OyIBQhbnPa68gnelNdN6_3k8wOucOiHGdVRX3Iva  军事训练与考核大纲编辑 本词条缺少信息栏、名片图，补充相关内容使词条更完整，还能快速升级，赶紧来编辑吧！ 《军事训练与考核大纲》经中国人民解放军总参谋部批准，正式颁发，自2009年1月1日起，全军和武警部队将按新大纲施训。新大纲的颁发，标志着中国人民解放军深化训练内容改革，成功构建了现阶段信息化条件下的军事训练内容体系，推进军事训练转变有了基础性的法规遵循。 目录 1简介 2历史 1简介编辑 新 大纲纵向分为联合训练和军兵种训练两个层面，横向包括诸军兵种专业。以提高联合作战能力为主线，按照军种战役、合同战术、分队训练和战技术训练基础构设训 练内容体系；拓展了非战争军事行动训练内容；增加了信息化知识技能和飞机、舰艇、导弹等高新武器装备模拟训练比重，并规范了网络训练、基地化训练和对抗训 练的方法步骤和标准；明确了复杂电磁环境下训练、夜间训练、复杂气象条件下训练以及心理承受能力训练的条件、形式、方法和要求；建立了以能力为核心的训练 标准和考评体系，细化和提高了基础训练标准，扩大了四级制考评范围，增设了百分制考评项目；确定了管理调控模式，规范了各级组训职责，明确了训练大纲的修 订权限。 据总参军训和兵种部有关负责人介绍，现行训练大纲是2001年颁发的。这次编修、试训和论证历时两年，千余名来自机关、部队、院校和科研机构的军事训练专家参与编修工作的指导、咨询和专题研究，各试训论证部队扎实组训，取得了一系列创新成果。[1-2]    2历史编辑 我军军事训练大纲编修史 ■从1957年开始，总参开始着手编修全军统一的训练大纲，谋求建立符合我军实际的训练内容体系。 ■1978年、1980年，全军两次编修训练大纲，主要是为了恢复正规的训练秩序，使训练大纲适应当时军事训练实际，内容更加完整系统。 ■1989年，为适应体制编制调整，全军第四次编修训练大纲，按军种分为陆军、海军、空军和第二炮兵四类，其中陆军训练大纲由总参颁发，其他由军种颁发。 ■1995年，为适应我军新时期战略方针，吸收部队训练改革成果，全军第五次编修训练大纲，分为陆、海、空、第二炮兵、国防科工委、武警部队六类，首次由总参统一颁发。 ■2001年，为适应兵役制度改革和科技练兵的需要，第六次编修训练大纲，这代大纲首次调整为军事训练与考核大纲， 分为陆、海、空、二炮、科研试验部队、预备役部队和武警部队七类，为建军以来编修册数最多、兵种专业最全，提出了诸如官兵分训、统放结合、质量调控、滚动 发展等一系列新的训练观，标志着我军对现代条件下军事训练的特点规律的认识达到了新境界。 ■2006年9月，为适应军事训练从机械化条件下向信息化条件下转变的需要，我军启动编修新一代训练和考核大纲。 2008年7月，新大纲颁发。这标志着我军成功构建了现阶段信息化条件下军事训练内容体系，推进军事训练转变有了基础性的法规遵循。自2009年1月1日 起，全军和武警部队将按新大纲施训。   http://www.baike.com/wiki/%E5%86%9B%E4%BA%8B%E8%AE%AD%E7%BB%83%E4%B8%8E%E8%80%83%E6%A0%B8%E5%A4%A7%E7%BA%B2  军事训练与考核大纲 点击认领  请用一段简单的话描述该词条，马上添加摘要。  目录 1简介  2历史  军事训练与考核大纲 - 简介  　　《军事训练与考核大纲》经中国人民解放军总参谋部批准，正式颁发，自2009年1月1日起，全军和武警部队将按新大纲施训。新大纲的颁发， 标志着中国人民解放军深化训练内容改革，成功构建了现阶段信息化条件下的军事训练内容体系，推进军事训练转变有了基础性的法规遵循。

2013 OMTE  http://military.people.com.cn/n/2015/0207/c1011-26524561.html   　由总参谋部牵头组织的全军新《军事训练与考核大纲（试训纲目）》集中会审日前落下帷幕。来自总部和各大单位训练部门的有关领导和专家，总结编修情况，交流纲目成果，研究重点问题，推进训练大纲由按科目设置内容向按任务设置内容转变，为构建实战化、科学化、规范化、标准化的训练内容体系奠定了坚实基础。记者从会审现场获悉，2015年全军将在各军区、军兵种、武警部队展开新纲目试训论证。  　　据总参军训部领导介绍，2013年4月新大纲编修工作启动以来，各单位坚决贯彻习主席重要指示和军委、总部决策部署，聚焦能打胜仗，贯彻真难严实要求，强化问题导向，坚持仗怎么打、大纲就怎么编，深入研究论证作战任务、武器装备、编制体制等对大纲的现实需求，广泛开展群众性理论研究、战法创新和专题攻关，紧贴实战编修现行大纲与实战不符的内容，取得阶段性成果。此次集中会审，主要是深入学习领会习主席关于实战化军事训练的重要论述，进一步统一编修思想、统一共性内容、统一体例格式、统一标准规范，部署试训论证任务。   http://news.mod.gov.cn/headlines/2015-02/07/content_4569245.htm  新训练大纲试训纲目完成集中会审 试训论证工作将在全军部队推开 来源：解放军报  作者：记者梁蓬飞、特约记者李玉明  时间：2015-02-07 08:31:51      本报讯 记者梁蓬飞、特约记者李玉明报道：由总参谋部牵头组织的全军新《军事训练与考核大纲 （试训纲目）》集中会审日前落下帷幕。来自总部和各大单位训练部门的有关领导和专家，总结编修情况，交流纲目成果，研究重点问题，推进训练大纲由按科目设 置内容向按任务设置内容转变，为构建实战化、科学化、规范化、标准化的训练内容体系奠定了坚实基础。记者从会审现场获悉，2015年全军将在各军区、军兵 种、武警部队展开新纲目试训论证。     据总参军训部领导介绍，2013年4月新大纲编修工作启动以来，各单位坚决贯彻习主席重要指示和军委、总部决策部署，聚焦能打胜仗，贯彻真 难严实要求，强化问题导向，坚持仗怎么打、大纲就怎么编，深入研究论证作战任务、武器装备、编制体制等对大纲的现实需求，广泛开展群众性理论研究、战法创 新和专题攻关，紧贴实战编修现行大纲与实战不符的内容，取得阶段性成果。此次集中会审，主要是深入学习领会习主席关于实战化军事训练的重要论述，进一步统 一编修思想、统一共性内容、统一体例格式、统一标准规范，部署试训论证任务。     记者翻看试训纲目感受到改革之风扑面而来，新大纲将在创新训练指导、优化内容设置、完善组训模式、发展考评体系、增大难度强度等方面发生很多变化。     ——紧贴我军使命任务，按照作战任务设计训练课题，按照实战环境设置训练条件，按照能力指标确立训练考评标准，将任务分解细化到训练大纲，实战味更浓了。     ——适应现代战争制胜机理，创新发展信息作战、精确作战、体系作战等训练内容，充实新装备新力量新领域训练，体现信息化局部战争的本质要求。     ——遵循战斗力生成规律，继承我军优良练兵传统，运用先进训练方法手段，进一步规范各层次训练组织实施，明确战术训练讲、研、摆、练方法，落实训练教学责任制。     ——坚持从难从严从实战需要出发，增大训练难度强度，创新战役战术标准，完善训练量化考评体系，推动训练质量评定由粗放向精细、由定性向定量转变。     记者了解到，从今年起，全军和武警部队将组织部分单位对新纲目进行试训验证，进一步创新联合训练、基地化训练、指挥训练、夜间训练等，同步推进训练法规、教材和保障条件等建设。     副总参谋长王建平、总参谋长助理陈勇参加会审并讲话。

PLA Political Work Regulations http://www.gov.cn/jrzg/2010-09/13/content_1701725.htm         https://baike.baidu.com/item/中国人民解放军政治工作条例/7480317  中国人民解放军政治工作条例  语音 编辑 讨论 上传视频 《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》2010年8月9日，经中国共产党中央委员会、中央军事委员会批准，中共中央发布。 中文名 中国人民解放军政治工作条例 发布单位 中共中央 最新版发布时间 2010年8月9日 发布版本 编辑 语音 中国人民解放军创建初期，中共中央为加强军队政治工作，借鉴苏联红军党政工作和中国国民革命军北伐战争时期政治工作的有关规定，于1930年10月发布了《中国工农红军政治工作暂行条例(草案)》。 1932年，在总结中国工农红军政治工作经验的基础上，经过广泛征求各方面意见，修订并重新发布了这一条例草案。 抗日战争时期，八路军总政治部于1938年制定发布了《国民革命军第十八集团军（第八路军）政治工作暂行条例(草案)》。 中华人民共和国成立后，为加强社会主义革命和社会主义建设时期的军队政治工作，中共中央、中央人民政府人民革命军事委员会于1954年4月15日发布了《中国人民解放军政治工作条例（草案）》，内容包括总则和中国共产党军队委员会、政治委员、总政治部、一级军区政治部、省军区政治部、军政治部、师政治部、团政治处、营政治教导员、政治协理员、连队政治指导员、中国共产党连队支部、中国新民主主义青年团连队支部、连队俱乐部、学校政治部（处）、海军舰艇部队政治工作、空军飞行大队政治工作、医院政治工作、革命军人代表会议条例等19个单项工作条例。 1963年3月27日经过修改，由中共中央重新发布，将军政治部工作条例和师政治部工作条例合为一个条例，增加了军分区政治部、县(市)人民武装部政治工作条例和连队革命军人委员会条例，撤销了革命军人代表会议条例和连队俱乐部工作条例。 1978年7月18日，中共中央重新发布《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》，增加了中国共产主义青年团团(营)工作委员会工作条例。随着形势的发展，为加强和改进军队政治工作，1983年12月，中国人民解放军总政治部修订印发了《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》（试行本），除原有单项条例外，增加了中国共产党机关支部工作条例。为适应社会主义现代化建设新时期军队建设的需要，1991、1995年，又两次重新发布了《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》。在1991年的条例中，在框架结构上作了较大变动，采用党章样式，将各个单项条例组合改变为一部条例的章节结构。 2003年12月5日，重新发布了《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》，对于规范和指导军队政治工作，加强部队党的建设和思想政治建设，推进政治工作创新发展，增强部队战斗力和保证圆满完成各项任务，发挥了重要作用。 2010年8月9日，经中共中央、中央军委批准，再一次重新发布《中国人民解放军政治工作条例》。新条例坚持以中国特色社会主义理论体系为指导，全面贯彻胡锦涛主席关于新形势下国防和军队建设重要论述，牢固确立科学发展观在国防和军队建设中的重要指导方针地位，广泛吸收部队创造的新经验新成果，积极回答和解决政治工作面临的新情况新问题，进一步规范了政治工作的指导思想、基本任务、主要内容、重要原则、方式方法和职责要求，为做好新形势下军队政治工作提供了基本遵循。 [1]  主要内容 编辑 语音 条例分为总则，政治工作的主要内容，总政治部，中国共产党在军队中的组织，中国共产党在军队中的纪律检查机关，政治委员、政治教导员和政治指导员，军区级以下单位政治机关，中国共产主义青年团在军队中的组织，军人代表会议和军人委员会，附则。共10章102条。 主要内容是：中国人民解放军是中国共产党缔造和领导，用马克思列宁主义、毛泽东思想和包括邓小平理论、“三个代表”重要思想以及科学发展观等重大战略思想在内的中国特色社会主义理论体系武装的人民军队，是中华人民共和国的武装力量，是人民民主专政的坚强柱石。紧紧地和人民站在一起，全心全意地为人民服务，是这支军队的唯一宗旨。中国人民解放军必须始终不渝地保持人民军队的性质，忠于党，忠于社会主义，忠于祖国，忠于人民。中国人民解放军的任务是：巩固国防，抵抗侵略，保卫祖国，保卫人民的和平劳动，参加国家建设事业。中国人民解放军在新世纪新阶段的历史使命是：为党巩固执政地位提供重要力量保证，为维护国家发展的重要战略机遇期提供坚强安全保障，为维护国家利益提供有力战略支撑，为维护世界和平与促进共同发展发挥重要作用。中国人民解放军必须高举中国特色社会主义伟大旗帜，坚持党的基本理论、基本路线、基本纲领和基本经验，贯彻毛泽东军事思想、邓

Cleared for public release 
September 2021 
 
 
 
The PLA’s New Joint Doctrine 
The Capstone of the New Era Operations Regulations System 
David M. Finkelstein 
 
 
  
 
Copyright © CNA 2021 
  
 
 
Abstract 
More than five years into an unprecedented series of reorganizations and systemic reforms which began in 2016, 
the Chinese People’s Liberation Army was issued new joint doctrine, the first update in 20 years. On November 13, 
2020, the Xinhua News Agency announced that the Chinese Communist Party’s Central Military Commission had 
issued the Guidelines on Joint Operations of the Chinese People’s Liberation Army (Trial). This paper presents what 
the PLA is saying about this new doctrinal guidance; places the joint operations Guidelines within the larger context 
of ongoing PLA reform and years of doctrinal evolution by drawing on previous work at the Center for Naval 
Analyses; engages in some informed speculation on various dimensions of the new joint doctrine; and identifies 
questions for further consideration. 
Author 
David M. Finkelstein is a Vice President at CNA and Director of the China & Indo-Pacific Security Affairs Division. 
He has been engaged in Asian security affairs for four decades, in and out of government, as a soldier, scholar, 
and analyst. A graduate of the United States Military Academy, he holds a PhD in Chinese and Japanese history 
from Princeton University and studied Mandarin at Nankai University in Tianjin, PRC. 
CNA’s Occasional Paper series is published by CNA, but the opinions expressed are those of the author(s) and do 
not necessarily reflect the views of CNA or the Department of the Navy.

text_snippet: PLA “Doctrine” and Theory A review of the 2000 to 2019 PRC Defense White Papers found several references to the English term “doctrine,” but the Chinese word was almost always lilun (理论), which normally translates as “theory” or “theoretical” throughout the defense white papers. The bottom line is that not a single PLA dictionary or encyclopedia has an English entry for “doctrine”. It does have multiple entries for “theory” (理论).  The PLAAF encyclopedia translates the USAF’s Basic Doctrine as 空军航空航天基本理论, which uses Lilun for “doctrine”. The last item in this paper shows other USAF doctrine documents translated into Chinese also – all of which use Lilun.  The 2009 version of US military JP 1-02 has the following definition for doctrine: Fundamental principles by which the military forces or elements thereof guide their actions in support of national objectives.  It is authoritative but requires judgment in application.  The 2019 version does not have a single definition for doctrine but does have the following definition for joint doctrine: Fundamental principles that guide the employment of United States military forces in coordinated action toward a common objective and may include terms, tactics, techniques, and procedures.  None of the JP 1-02 versions have the term theory in them.  The Chinese translate the US Army Training and Doctrine Command (TRADOC) as the US Army Training and Regulations Command (美国陆军训练与条令司令部) 2019 Defense White Paper The strategic goals for the development of China’s national defense and military in the new era are: • to comprehensively advance the modernization of military theory, organizational structure, military personnel, and weaponry and equipment in step with the modernization of the country and basically complete the modernization of national defense and the military by 2035; 新时代中国国防和军队建设的战略目标是，到2020年基本实现机械化，信息化建设取得重大进展，战略能力有大的提升。同国家现代化进程相一致，全面推进军事理论现代化、军队组织形态现代化、军事人员现代化、武器装备现代化，力争到2035年基本实现国防和军队现代化，到本世纪中叶把人民军队全面建成世界一流军队。 Promoting

Hu Jintao Military Thought http://www.ycwb.com/xkb/2007-08/02/content_1569628.htm  胡锦涛讲话体现其“三战”治军思想  新快报讯　据香港《文汇报》报道，中国人民解放军建军80周年暨全军英雄模范代表大会昨天举行，中共中央总书记、国家主席、中央军委主席胡锦涛在会上发表重要讲话。胡锦涛刚柔相济、缓急有度的卓越表现已经获得国内外广泛好评。在军事方面，其备战、慎战、 敢战的“三战”军事思想也已逐渐体现出来。  我国军事专家表示，胡锦涛关于国防建设的基本战略思想是：既不轻易诉诸武力，又要做到敢于应战；要处理好备战、慎战和敢战的关系，不战则已，战则必胜。胡锦涛军事思想继承和发展了毛泽东的军事思想，是科学的、辩证的、与时俱进的思想。  中国军事科学院副院长刘继贤不久前解读胡锦涛军事思想时指出，胡锦涛“慎战”与“敢战”是统一的，“慎战”不是“怯战”，“敢战”不是“好战”。两者的统一就是最大限度地避免战争，一旦战争不可避免，就要确保能赢得战争，也就是不战则已，战则必胜。而“慎战”和“敢战”的前提均是“备战”。  http://theory.people.com.cn/GB/10266861.html    http://www.people.com.cn/GB/32306/33232/4865020.html

Chapter Eleven: The People’s Liberation Army Second Artillery Force (PLASAF) as an Organization  Michael S. Chase, Daniel Yoon, and Mark Stokes  Introduction As part of the ongoing modernization of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA), the People’s Republic of China (PRC) has clearly prioritized the improvement of its ballistic and cruise missile capabilities. Indeed, the transformation of the PLA Second Artillery Force (PLASAF/第二炮兵/二炮), which as China’s strategic missile force is responsible for most of its conventional and nuclear ballistic missiles and land-attack cruise missiles (LACMs) — has emerged as one of the centerpieces of Chinese military modernization. In less than two decades, China has progressed from a limited and vulnerable, non-mobile nuclear ballistic missile capability to one of the world’s most impressive nuclear and conventional ballistic missile, as well as cruise missile, programs. The transformation of Second Artillery is underscored by an April 2009 U.S. National Air and Space Intelligence Center (NASIC) report, which concludes that China now has “the most active and diverse ballistic missile development program in the world.” In addition, China is “developing and testing offensive missiles, forming additional missile units, qualitatively upgrading certain missile systems, and developing methods to counter ballistic missile defenses.”   The past decade has witnessed significant changes in PLASAF’s organizational structure. The number of missile bases has remained constant, but as the NASIC report indicates, there has been an increase in the number of brigades as Second Artillery has formed new missile units. text_snippet: Second Artillery Equipment Research Academy  装备研究院军事理论与作战应用研究所伪装与防护技术研究室  装备研究院某所六室, 室党支部  装备研究院某研究所, 研究室党支部  装备研究院财务军需处  二炮装备研究院中试与检测中心  二炮装备研究院审价中心  http://bbs.kaoyan.com/t2915791p1  第二炮兵装备研究院科技部  http://www.chinamil.com.cn/site1/zbxl/2008-04/22/content_1214458.htm  第二炮兵装备研究院一批新型模拟训练器材及时发送部队     本 报讯　特约通讯员周明、记者张旗报道：3月中旬，由第二炮兵装备研究院研制的一批新型模拟训练器材发送到基层部队。前来领取新型模拟训练器材的某旅一营营 长王东高兴地对记者说：“有了它们，我们就能放开手脚训，尽快让新装备形成战斗力。”据该院科技部副部长鲁艾介绍，近20套主战和保障装备的新型模拟训练 器材目前已研制完毕，年底之前陆续发送到基层部队。     近年来，大量新装备列装部队。在短时间内让新装备形成战斗力成为当务之急。然而，由于没 有等效的模拟训练器材，部队训练只能依赖实装操作，这使新装备的损耗加速，其作战效能和使用寿命受到了影响。急部队之所急，该装备研究院派出80余名科研 人员到基层部队跟踪训练，根据训练实际需求，研制开发了一批新型模拟训练器材。在该装备研究院样品仓库，记者看到，集声、光、电、磁为一体的某新型模拟训 练器材，可对每一次训练进行数据记录和精确打分。     某旅反馈的信息显示，利用新型模拟训练器材开展训练一个月来，装备损耗率大大下降，训练时间增加了3倍，总体成绩上升了15%。 来源：解放军报第11版 发布时间： 2008-04-22 06:37)     http://news.mod.gov.cn/forces/2007-12/18/content_3052479_3.htm  第二炮兵装备研究院探讨自主创新 来源：解放军报第3版  作者：本报记者　夏洪青　本报特约记者　王永孝　本报特约通讯员　周明  时间：2007-12-18 06:17:26     结合学习十七大精神，院党委反躬自省：我们平时强调多出成果、快出成果比较多，从而出现了以“科研奖项论英雄”的现象。他们重新修订了科研 干部考评机制，把科研成果的作战应用效果作为检验科研绩效的一道“硬杠杠”。出台了《推荐国家和军队科学技术奖励实施办法》等规定，使科研课题立项、招 标、报奖进一步规范化、透明化。     失败300多次的试验该不该继续做下去？     博士工程师吕永胜承担“变色龙”材料的一个项目试验，他反复进行了多次试验，均告失败。历经3年，在第357次试验时获得成功。     对于这个“傻子”，当时议论的人很多：“失败了300多次还搞，这不是浪费国家的钱财吗？”     讨论中，政治部主任于增泉讲了个“反例”：某研究室新来的一位博士，一次科研攻关失败后，单位领导逢会就批，结果，这位本来很有潜力的高学历人才被弄得几年抬不起头来，更谈不上专心搞科研了。     对照十七大报告，大家自然扯起了“进一步营造鼓励创新的环境”这个话题。为什么有的科研干部缺乏敢冒风险、敢为人先的胆识呢？为什么有的科研干部有创新点子却不敢提出来呢？究其原因，是缺乏鼓励创新、宽容失败的氛围。     院党委达成共识：科研本身是有风险的，要区分两种不同的失败，一种失败是不努力、不作为引起的，而另一种失败，则是科研攻关中很难避免的一个途径。对前者，要坚决批评；对于后者，则要给予理解和支持。 http://www.zhgpl.com/doc/1003/5/4/1/100354114.html?coluid=4&kindid=18&docid=100354114

Chapter Nine: The People’s Liberation Army Navy as an Evolving Organization  Nan Li  Introduction This chapter examines the People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) as an evolving organization. It addresses three sets of research questions. The first set deals with the issue of headquarters-level concepts and command, control, and coordination. Specifically, what are the PLAN’s roles and missions? What constitutes the PLAN’s leadership and administrative and functional and departments (海军领导机关), and what are their grades and ranks? How does the Party Committee decision style reconcile with the need for “singularly exercising power” (“专一事权”) for a military organization such as the PLAN, and what is the relationship between the navy leadership and the PLAN headquarter’s first-level departments (一级部)? What organizational changes have occurred in the PLAN Headquarters’ first-level departments, and what subordinate departments constitute them? What are the selection trends of the PLAN leadership?  The second set of questions has to do with the PLAN below the headquarters-level, including the fleet, base, and zhidui levels. What are the major combatant, research, and professional military education (PME) organizations that constitute the PLAN? What are their grades and ranks? What major organizational changes have been introduced to them?   The third set of questions concerns the relationship between the evolution of the PLAN as an organization and China’s evolving naval strategy, as well as the relationship of the PLAN with other elements of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) and defense industry organizations, mainly in terms of joint operations and naval equipment and armament development. How has the PLAN evolved with the changing Chinese naval strategy? What organizational changes have been introduced to enhance the coordination between the PLAN and non-PLAN organizations for joint operations and for naval equipment and armament development? The director is Wang Yu (汪玉) http://mil.news.sina.com.cn/2006-09-18/0635398883.html  92537部队潜艇研究室副主任缪旭弘  http://xsjw.nbxs.gov.cn/show_info.php?from=2&id=2478  习近平：共产党员要做时代先锋为党旗添彩 发布时间：2009-07-02 09:12:30  浏览次数：105   放军92537部队部队长汪玉 http://www.cn3x.com.cn/zt/content/2012-04/06/content_267292.htm  海军装备研究院院长汪玉。 　　【人物档案】 　　汪玉，湖北枝江人，海军少将，海军装备研究院院长，博士、研究员、博士生导师，第十、十一届全国人大代表，我国舰船系统工程领域著名专家。 　　1980年毕业于夷陵中学，并考入上海交通大学动力机械工程专业，1987年12月从该校研究生毕业，入伍分配到原海军装备论证中心，现更名为 海军装备研究院。28岁时破格晋升为高级工程师，30岁享受政府特殊津贴，34岁担任研究所总工程师，39岁当选为十届全国人大代表。主持完成40余项国 家和军队重点科研项目，先后获得国家科技进步二等奖3项，国际发明展览会金奖1项，军队科技进步一等奖7项、二等奖8项，获授权国防发明专利16项，出版 学术著作8部，在国内外期刊和学术会议上发表论文90余篇。 　　因成绩突出，先后荣获中国青年科技奖、军队专业技术重大贡献奖、第七届“发明创业奖”特等奖并被授予“当代发明家”荣誉称号，入选国家“百千万 人才工程”第一、二层次人选。多次被评为全国优秀共产党员、全国优秀科技工作者，是军队高层次科技创新人才工程科技领军人才培养对象，享受政府特殊津贴和 军队优秀专业技术人才一类岗位津贴。先后荣立一等功1次、二等功4次，两次受到军委通令表彰，并多次受到胡锦涛主席等党和国家、军队领导人的接见。 　　感谢母校给我“隐形的翅膀” 　　走进夷陵中学校园，教学楼前的花园里有一块偌大的石头，“饮水思源”四个大字在其上分外夺目。这块石头是汪玉和他当年夷陵中学同窗校友共同捐赠 的。“那是2010年7月31日，正值我们从母校毕业30年。”汪玉欣然接受了记者的采访。他说，同学们放下各自手中的事务，从世界各地回到了魂牵梦绕、 阔别30年的母校。当时正值网上热议华科大的根叔和重大的李哥“关于母校”的诠释和感悟。但他却觉得一直以来，夷陵中学在他和同学们的心目中，不仅仅是一 所求知的学校，更是大家共同的精神家园和神圣的净土。那里印刻着他们成长的记忆，记录着他们从懵懂青涩少年一路走来的人生起步历程。 　　汪玉回忆说，1978年从白洋镇普溪窑中学考入夷陵中学时，学校面向宜昌地区只招生两个班。同学们大都来自农村，不仅成绩非常优秀，而且特别勤 奋。“正是从曾经朝夕相伴、共渡学海的同窗好友身上，我学到了什么是勤奋和坚韧；从老师深夜教学楼窗前批改作业时弯弯的背影里，我知道了什么是承诺和呵 护。”为了实现自己的梦想，他和后来考取清华大学的季林红同学约定彼此监督，互相激励，每天提前1小时起床学习。有一次，他俩起床早读了很长时间，仍不见 天亮，不得不回到寝室又睡了一觉。谈到这个细节时，汪玉笑了。他说，那时没有闹钟，也没有手表，一切作息依据就是校园里的铃声。高考时，季林红和汪玉仅以 1分之差，分别夺得宜昌地区第一名和第二名。填写报考志愿时，本来可以与季林红一起上清华大学的汪玉选择了上海交通大学。“是母校让我们找到了开启人生的 第一把钥匙，铸就了划动生命之舟的第一支船桨，长出了搏击长空的第一对‘隐形的翅膀’。”在同学聚会上，汪玉被大家推举为发言人。他动情地说，岁月可以带 走他们青春的容颜，却带不走对母校、老师的眷恋，冲不淡师生、同窗之间的深情厚谊。无论大家有着怎样的经历和成就，都只有一个共同的名字，那就是同学。无 论他们遍尝多少人生百味，都有着在夷陵相濡以沫的共同经历……一番肺腑之言，说得刚刚聚首的80多名同窗热泪盈眶。大家相约，无论怎样变迁、身处何地，都 要把握好生命中每一个宝贵的当下，共同开创夷陵中学、每一位夷陵中学人的美好未来。 　　两次放弃出国机会选择“蓝海” 　　“如果说第一次选择是因为兴趣，那么，后两次选择是因为使命。”回首以往，汪玉坦言也曾面对无数次的选择，但刻骨铭心的只有三次。高考填报志愿 时，他因为从小向往蓝天，而放弃了清华大学，选择到上海交通大学。硕士研究生毕业时，适逢“出国热”、“经商热”，既可以选择出国深造，也

Su Shiliang http://www.hudong.com/wiki/%E8%8B%8F%E5%A3%AB%E4%BA%AE  苏士亮 开放分类： 人物 军事领域人物 政治人物  苏士亮，男，1950年9月生，山东青州人。 编辑摘要  苏士亮 - 简介  苏士亮，1950年9月生，山东青州人。历任海军驱逐舰第一支队支队长，海军青岛基地参谋长，北海舰队副参谋长，海军指挥学院院长，南京军区副参谋长，2006年5月任济南军区副司令员兼北海舰队司令员。2007年12月出任广州军区副司令员兼南海舰队司令员，是十七届中央候补委员，海军中将军衔。2008年1月任广州军区副司令员、南海舰队司令员。2008年12月出席了海军护航舰艇编队出征索马里海域执行护航任务动员大会。

In April 1952, the PLAN formally established the Naval Aviation Department with 318 personnel as one of six separate administrative departments within PLAN Headquarters. Dun Xingyun became the commander and Li Keru the political commissar. Naval Aviation also became one of the PLAN’s five operational branches (bingzhong), along with the submarine, surface, marine corps, and coastal defense branches.  Besides school closings, the Cultural Revolution had disastrous consequences across the board for Naval Aviation. The primary person responsible for Naval Aviation’s demise was the PLAN’s political commissar, Li Zuopeng, who was part of Defense Minister Lin Biao’s clique. Li became one of the PLAN’s deputy commanders in 1962 and was promoted to political commissar position in January 1967. As the political commissar, Li basically ran the PLAN until he was arrested immediately following Lin Biao’s plane crash over Mongolia in September 1971. Besides closing Naval Aviation schools, Li abolished Naval Aviation headquarters and its entire command staff in November 1969. Therefore, aviation units within the fleets were left to themselves without guidance from the PLAN headquarters. The headquarters was not reestablished until May 1978.

THE PEOPLE’S LIBERATION ARMY NAVY  AS AN EVOLVING ORGANIZATION  Nan Li  Introduction This chapter examines the People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) as an evolving organization. It addresses three sets of research questions. The first set deals with the issue of service-level concepts and command and control. Specifically, what are the PLAN’s roles and missions? What constitutes the PLAN’s leadership and administrative and functional and departments (海军领导机关), and what are their grades and ranks? How does the Party Committee decision style reconcile with the need for “singularly exercising power” (“专一事权”) for a military organization such as the PLAN, and what is the relationship between the navy leadership and the PLAN headquarters’ first-level departments (一级部)? What organizational changes have occurred in the PLAN’s first-level departments, and what subordinate departments constitute them? What are the selection trends of the PLAN leadership? The second set of questions has to do with the PLAN below the service-level headquarters. text_snippet: China’s Aircraft Carrier Liaoning CO: Zhang Zheng  http://chn.chinamil.com.cn/jdtp/2012-09/25/content_5038673.htm  我国第一艘航空母舰舰长和政委亮相 作者：查春明 2012-09-25 17:35    来源：新华社    http://news.qq.com/a/20120926/000376.htm  航母舰长张峥浙江造 父亲也是海军军官 2012年09月26日07:49钱江晚报我要评论(55)  字号：T|T  我国第一艘航空母舰“辽宁舰”已按计划完成建造和试验试航工作，25日上午在中国船舶重工集团公司大连造船厂正式交付海军。中共中央总书记、国家主席、中央军委主席胡锦涛出席交接入列仪式并登舰视察。 中国第一艘航空母舰“辽宁舰”舰长张峥，海军大校军衔，研究生学历，曾任护卫舰、驱逐舰长，并曾赴英国军事院校留学。 张峥是浙江人。1969年，张峥出生于浙江长兴，父亲也是海军军官，后随父定居浙江舟山，在舟山定海一中完成初中、高中的学习。 昨晚，本报记者赶到张峥高三班主任黄老师家中，听她讲述高中时期张峥的一些事。黄老师回忆，当年的张峥身材瘦小，走路、说话时腰板却挺得笔直；不爱说话，却喜欢默默帮助别人；解开疑难问题时，紧锁的眉头自然解开；最常穿的衣服，是草绿色的军装…… 黄启英老师今年62岁，6年前退休，当了一辈子老师。1986年8月，教政治的黄老师接手张峥所在的定海一中高三（2）班，任班主任。这个班级一共有42名同学，张峥任副班长兼学习委员。 在黄老师眼里，两种学生最能让她记住：一种是品学兼优的；另一种是调皮捣蛋的。黄老师说，张峥毫无疑问属于第一种。 黄老师记得，张峥是班上为数不多的在军营里长大的孩子，虽然能说一口流利的舟山话，平时却很少和别的同学聊天、说闲话。 高考结束后，同学们回到教室填报志愿。张峥让黄老师留下了最深刻的印象。 “几乎每个同学都填满了3个志愿大学，3项可调剂的专业。”黄老师说，唯独张峥，只填了一所大学，一项志愿，而且不服从志愿调剂。 那就是之后张峥就读的大学——上海交通大学自动控制系。 “当时，我还特地找到他，希望他多填几个志愿，以防万一。”黄老师说，张峥都拒绝了。 “考试一结束，我问过张峥，考得怎么样，他一再只用‘还好’回答。”黄老师说，从这件事上，她更以张峥是自己的学生而自豪：谦虚的态度下，装着满满的自信。 “他还告诉我，填报这个专业，就是为了以后能进部队。”这时，黄老师才想起，她认识张峥一年来，张峥最常穿的衣服，总是草绿色的，那是军装的颜色。 高中毕业后，班级组织过几次同学会，但是，每次张峥都因为工作忙，没赶上参加。“他给我打过几次电话，包括进了部队，第一时间告诉我了。”黄老师说。 黄老师最近一次见到张峥是2005年。“他来学校看我，我们聊了很久。”黄老师说，那时，她才第一次发现，张峥能说那么多话。 结束采访前，黄老师将张峥的一张高中毕业照送给了《钱江晚报》。“希望他越做越好，成为世界级的航母舰长，保卫祖国的蓝色家园。”黄老师希望借助我们，寄语张峥。 http://www.nbd.com.cn/mobile/articles/2012-09-26/684769.html  辽宁舰舰长张峥来自海军东海舰队 曾在英国深造（组图） 2012-09-26 11:50 来源: 人民网  核心提示：“辽宁舰”正式交付海军，最自豪的当属首批舰员。首任舰长张峥曾担任护卫舰、驱逐舰舰长，与浙江有着特殊的缘分。1969年，张峥出生于浙江长兴，后随父定居浙江舟山，张峥在舟山定海一中完成初中、高中的学习。 辽宁舰舰长张峥来自海军东海舰队 曾在英国深造（组图） “辽宁舰”正式交付海军，最自豪的当属首批舰员。首任舰长张峥曾担任护卫舰、驱逐舰舰长，与浙江有着特殊的缘分。1969年，张峥出生于浙江长兴，后随父定居浙江舟山，张峥在舟山定海一中完成初中、高中的学习。 张峥来自海军东海舰队，曾任某支队副参谋长。公开资料显示，张峥，1969年出生在军人家庭，大学毕业后分配到海军东海舰队司令部，任助理工程师。 随后在海军大连舰艇学院就读舰艇作战指挥专业，获硕士学位。此后先后担任部门长、参谋、副舰长、舰长。 此外，张峥曾先后到英国国防语言学院和英国三军联合指挥与参谋学院深造。 归类于: 媒体聚焦每日精选头条探索曝光台 来源: 人民网 责任编辑: 靳水平   WCT: Zhang Zheng Named First Commander of the Liaoning CPP20120926968164 filename: Recommended Edits to Nan Li's Paper.docx
text_snippet: Recommended Edits for Nan Li’s Paper Basically, this entire paper needs to be completely re-written concerning all references to organizational structure, ranks, and grades. Need a list of acronyms. DC is not a valid acronym for deputy commander. Please review ONI’s China’s Navy 2007, and use ONI’s approved terminology. You can cite this in an early footnote. Do not use “organ”. Use administrative and functional departments or simply departments.  Use zhidui, dadui, and zhongdui. Do not use detachment for zhidui. Refer them to the Intro chapter Appendix A for terminology if necessary. Do not use PLAN Armament Department. Use Equipment Department. Do not use “army grade”. It is corps leader or corps deputy leader Do not use deputy MR/corps/division/regiment grade...use MR/corps/division deputy leader grade There is no “brigade” grade. Brigade leaders are division deputy leader-grade officers. Do not use “级” for grade, use “职”. Be consistent: use Party Committee vs party committee and Standing Committee vs standing committee. Use Party not party Consistency: anytime you have the full name of a department, it needs to be capitalized as in Headquarters Department, Political Department, Logistics Department, and Equipment Department Each level has a leader (正) and deputy (副) leader grade, so do not use “division level” as it can be division leader or deputy leader.  You are consistently confusing your audience by citing only one rank per grade. Every grade has two ranks—a primary and secondary rank. Furthermore, the PLAN does not use “RADM upper half and lower half”. This is a USN term. PLAN rear admirals have only one star. The best thing to do is refer your readers to the Intro chapter that explains that each grade has two ranks...a primary and secondary. Page 4, para 2: Change “the four staff departments, namely command, political, logistics, and armament department” to read “the four first-level departments, namely Headquarters, Political, Logistics, and Equipment...

China’s Defense Minister and Ministry of National Defense
Kenneth Allen, Christopher M. Clarke, and John Corbett
The purpose of this chapter is to provide an overview of the People’s Republic of China’s 
(PRC’s) Defense Minister (国防部长) and Ministry of National Defense (MND / 国防部). 
Despite decades of research, dozens of books, and hundreds of articles on the People’s Liberation 
Army (PLA / 人民解放军), our examination of the literature has turned up no adequate and 
complete study of MND’s history, structure, functions, and actual operation, especially in the 
post-Mao era. For example, no official Chinese government or military publications or websites 
provides details about MND’s organizational structure and responsibilities or clarity about the 
Defense Minister’s responsibilities.1  It is our hope that this chapter will encourage future 
research to close the obvious information gaps on this important organization and PLA leader. 
Based on the information available, this chapter addresses these issues in the following sections 
and appendices:
•
Section 1: Introduction
•
Section 2: Defense Minister and MND Grades
•
Section 3: Defense Minister Responsibilities
•
Section 4: MND Responsibilities and Organizational Structure
•
Section 5: Possible Reforms
•
Section 6: Information Gaps
•
Appendix A: Ministry of National Defense History
•
Appendix B: China’s 10 Defense Ministers
•
Appendix C: Defense Minister Travel Abroad 2003-2012
•
Defense Minister Hosted Meetings with Foreign Military Leaders 2008-2012
Section 1: Introduction
The first step in understanding the roles of China’s Defense Minister and the Ministry of 
National Defense is to recognize that they do not equate in any respect to the United States’ 
Secretary of Defense and Department of Defense in terms of making defense policy or 
commanding the military. In China, the Chinese Communist Party’s (CCP’s) Central Military 
Commission (CMC / 中央军委) and the four General Departments—General Staff Department 
(GSD)

Chapter Six: General Logistics Department Organizational Reforms: 2000 – 2012  Erin Richter, Leigh Ann Ragland, and Katherine Atha    Assessments and measurements of the PLA’s success or failure should avoid simplistic and direct comparisons to other modern military logistics systems, especially evolving Western logistics systems. Analysts should avoid assessments based on perennially limited information that habitually disdain PLA logistics as a historically weak link. On the contrary, PLA logistics have proven highly adaptable and flexible to the situation. Often PLA logistics was not “pretty.”  It lacked efficiency, expended excessive personnel and other resources, or it failed to support decisive engagements. Significant shortfalls do exist. Nonetheless, PLA logistics has been proficient in providing sufficient operational support to massive numbers of personnel and equipment of mixed generations and origins that would make Western logisticians blanch.  Susan Puska, “The People’s Liberation Army (PLA) General Logistics Department(GLD): Towards Joint Logistics Support 2000.”  Introduction This chapter follows the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) General Logistics Department (GLD) (总后勤部) from the 2002 The People’s Liberation Army as Organization conference. The 2002 chapter on the GLD, along with several other publications, provided a good foundation for examining the historical background of the GLD’s organizational structure. However, while researching the GLD’s organization, we discovered that the majority of authoritative sources on the GLD are dated 2003 or earlier. Many documents draw heavily on the People’s Liberation Army Military Logistics151 Encyclopedia published in 2002, which predates major GLD reorganizations that began in 2003. At this time, no openly available publication, in English or Chinese, comprehensively captures the organizational changes within the GLD. General Armament Department  Kevin Pollpeter and Amy Chang  The General Armament Department (GAD/总装备部), also referred to as the General Armaments Department and the General Equipment Department (GED), was created in 1998 and is the youngest of the four General Departments. The GAD is a Central Military Commission (CMC) member–grade organization (军委委员) and is led by a Director (主任) who holds the same grade and has the rank of general. The GAD also has a political commissar (政委), who serves as the secretary of the Party Committee and is a military region (MR) leader-grade (正大军区职) officer who holds the rank of lieutenant general or general. In addition, the GAD has averaged three to five deputy directors and one deputy political commissar, who, like the political commissar, have the grade of military region leader and the rank of lieutenant general or general. To date, no PLA Navy, Air Force, or Second Artillery officer has served as a deputy.   The GAD is responsible for the PLA’s weapons and equipment research, development, acquisition, and maintenance. It determines, formulates, supervises, and implements the policies, laws and regulations regarding weapons and equipment for the entire military. The GAD is also responsible for weapons and equipment budgeting, including oversight and auditing. In conducting these tasks, the GAD operates China’s test, evaluation, and training bases as well as a network of military representative bureaus and offices. Finally, the GAD guides the direction of PLA modernization through its Science and Technology Committee.   The GAD was formed in April 1998 from elements of the General Staff Department (GSD), the General Logistics Department (GLD), and the now defunct Commission for Science, Technology, and Industry for National Defense (COSTIND/国防科学技术工业委员/科工委) in an effort to establish more military control over weapons and equipment development and procurement. Academy of Equipment Command and Technology (装备指挥技术学院) The Academy of Equipment Command Technology is an institution aimed at the development of weaponry and equipment, training command and management personnel, training senior engineering and technical personnel for national defense scientific research and applications. It was founded in 1978 and changed to its present name in 1999 (formerly known as COSTIND Command Institute of Technology). The academy is located in Huairou, Beijing. The academy has a testing and launch site, TT&C facility, electro-optical tracking measurement facility, command and control facility, and a communications facility. Some of the major fields of study include: aircraft testing and launching, computer and applications, TT&C engineering, communication engineering, optoelectronic measuring equipment, radio telemetry remote control, conventional weapons testing equipment, and aerospace communications.  Armored Corps Engineering Academy (装甲兵工程学院) Armored Corps Engineering Academy is a professional and technical institution to train armored equipment and technical support personnel for the military’s armored mechanized units. This college was founded in 1953 and is formerly known as the Harbin Military Engineering College Armored Forces Department. In 1961 it was expanded to Xi’an and was called the Armored Forces Engineering College and in 1969 it was moved to Beijing.  The academy has 30 fields of study and has specialized laboratories, field training ground and other training simulation centers, a computer center, and language laboratory. It has close to 200 laboratories (including one National Key Laboratory), a computer center, an electro-optical measurement center, a combat simulation center, and a teaching command center. It has one Ph.D. science and research post-doctoral flow station, and 31 research areas. General Armament Department 总装备部  Kevin Pollpeter and Amy Chang  The General Armament Department (GAD), established in 1998, is the youngest of the four general departments. The GAD is responsible for weapons and equipment development and maintenance, and operating China’s test, evaluation, and training bases. The GAD also guides the direction of PLA modernization through its Science and Technology Committee, an organization with a direct channel to the CMC.  The GAD was formed in April 1998 from the now defunct civilian Commission on Science, Technology, and Industry for National Defense (COSTIND) in an effort to establish more military control over weapons and equipment development and procurement. This reform abolished COSTIND and placed responsibility for procurement in the hands of the GAD. The previous procurement bureaucracy made up of COSTIND and the GLD had been characterized by competing interests. COSTIND, for example, was widely seen as representing the defense industry’s interests over the military’s interests. The new structure sought to correct this deficiency by centralizing control and decision-making in the hands of the newly formed GAD and relegated COSTIND to the diminished, but still important, role of managing the defense industry.  The GAD-COSTIND relationship was further reformed in 2008 at the expense of COSTIND. While it appears that GAD did not directly benefit from this latest reform, COSTIND was downgraded to the State Administration for Science, Technology, and Industry for National Defense (SASTIND), a ju-level organization.  With this reorganization, responsibility for direct management of the affairs of the defense industry was given to the State Assets Supervision and Administration Commission (SASAC). Chapter Twelve: China’s People’s Armed Police Force Leadership, Command, and Organization in the Wake of the 2009 PAP Law  Murray Scot Tanner  Important as the development of the regular PLA has been to China’s emergence as a global power, China’s ability to develop effective, professional paramilitary internal security and emergency response forces will almost certainly have a greater impact on China’s day-to-day national security than any other effort to strengthen China’s military and security forces.  The Chinese Communist Party (CCP) continues to face increasing social unrest, violent crime, cross-border drug trafficking, and other threats to internal stability. Moreover, it has been made clear by recent major social uprisings and natural disasters, such as the 2008 and 2009 riots in Lhasa and Urumqi, and the 2008 Sichuan earthquake, that when China’s paramilitary and emergency response forces are unable to handle these crises, Beijing has little choice but to call upon the regular PLA to help support these disaster relief and domestic security operations. China’s capacity to develop emergency response and paramilitary forces, therefore, will continue to have a major impact on the regular PLA’s ability to free itself to focus on its new emerging missions.  This chapter examines recent efforts to reorganize People’s Armed Police Force (PAP/武装警察部队/武警部队) leadership and management to help it better meet the leadership’s expectations of this force.  It focuses in particular on the most important effort to reorganize PAP leadership and command in the past decade—the passage in 2009 of the Law of the People’s Republic of China on the People’s Armed Police (hereafter the PAP Law)—which raised serious issues of the ability of local officials to mobilize the PAP for security missions in their area. The organizational and grade structure, including first- and second-level departments, is the same as that for the MR, PLAN, PLAAF, and PLASAF Headquarters.  PAP Headquarters (总部) is an MR leader-grade (3) organization The Headquarters Department and Political Department are MR deputy leader-grade (4) organizations The Logistics Department and Equipment Department are corps leader-grade (5) organizations Corps leader-grade (5) organizations Beijing and Xinjiang zongdui (总队) Gold (黄金), Hydropower (水电), Communications (交通), and Forestry (森林) zhihuibu (指挥部) Corps deputy leader-grade (6) organizations All other province, municipality, and autonomous region zongdui (各省、直辖市、自治区武警总队) Division leader-grade (7) organizations 14 mobile divisions (机动师), which are four-digit 86xx and 87xx organizations Gold, Hydropower, Communications, and Forestry zongdui The Gold and Hydropower zongdui are numbered 1st to 3rd (e.g., 黄金第一总队) The Communications zongdui are numbered 1st to 2nd Regiment leader-grade (9) organizations Subordinate zhidui (支队) to each type of division leader grade-organization noted above The Intro chapter for this book also has the following: Zhihui bu (指挥部): This term is often translated as command, but is best left as zhihui bu. text_snippet: Comments on GSD Paper There are too many terms that do not have the Chinese characters after them, such as NUDT. They are just put in on a random basis.  The font for all Chinese characters should be 12 font not 10 font.  The first word in all bulleted items should be capitalized  There is no mention of protocol order in the entire paper. Each section and subsection should at least speculate about the protocol order. It appears that some Chinese characters are not in SimSun but use another font Each subsection on a certain department should have a figure showing the subordinate orgs.  Several times, there is speculation about what a certain organization could do, but no footnotes or explanation as to who says it or at least give an example of why they think it. See page 10, second paragraph. The font is not even throughout certain paragraphs, such as the next to last para on page 9. There are multiple acronyms, such as TRB, that have not been spelled out at any point in the paper. Page 1, Para 1: Should make the point that, although the 1998 Defense White Paper used General Staff Department, every white paper since then has used General Staff Headquarters, but the USG calls it GSD, as will this paper. The paper should address why the PLA chose to change the English, as it apparently has some significance.  The intro should state that this chapter updates the 2002 volume specifically. Page 1, Para 2: GSD, which is a first-level department, ...... It is not a “general executive office, it is a General Office (办公厅). I have never seen it called the former. The Personality Director also uses the latter. Even the first sentence under Leadership uses General Office.   Is it “The GSD” or just “GSD”? For the most part, it should just be GSD.....and use the GSD when it has something following it like the GSD Operations Department or The GSD’s ...as in the GSD’s leadership...  It is Deputy Chiefs and Assistant Chiefs of the General Staff not Deputy Chiefs and Assistant chiefs

filename: PLA as Organization Reference Volume 2 web intro v2.docx
text_snippet: PLA as Organization v 2.0  The time period since the first PLA as Organization conference was held in August 2000 and the subsequent publication of the conference volume in 2002 has seen an explosion in the number of resources, especially Internet resources, with which to analyze China’s Ministry of National Defense (MND), People’s Liberation Army (PLA), and People’s Armed Police (PAP). With these organizations undergoing a rapid modernization and changing their organizational structure to meet the demands of a new generation of weapons and equipment, as well as fighting and winning wars under informatized conditions, the ten year anniversary of the publication of the PLA as Organization Reference Volume v1.0 was an ideal time to take another look at the very important, but little covered topic of the MND’s, PLA’s, and PAP’s organizational structure.  From June 13-14, 2012, DGI organized a conference with some of the best minds on MND, PLA, and PAP organization issues providing their knowledge as paper writers, discussants, and conference attendees. This volume presents the outcome of that conference and includes the organizational and personnel changes that occurred in conjunction with the 18th Party Congress in November 2012 and has been updated through mid-2014. This work builds upon the foundation laid in the 2002 volume by including a chapter on the MND. It is not only an essential reference volume for cataloging changes in the organizational structure of the PLA and PAP since the first conference volume was published, but also sets an important baseline for understanding the major structural reforms of the PLA and PAP scheduled to occur before 2020.

Chapter Four: The Chinese People’s Liberation Army General Staff Department: Evolving Organization and Missions  Mark A. Stokes and Ian Easton   Introduction The Chinese People’s Liberation Army (PLA) is transforming into a modern military force capable of an increasingly diverse set of missions further from its shores. The General Staff Department (GSD/总参某部/总参), which was created in March 1931, is the heart of the PLA and driver of its future. GSD develops policies, plans, and programs, establishes requirements, and allocates resources to support the PLA mission to defend the interests of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP). The GSD is responsible for day to day joint operations, intelligence, strategic planning and operational requirements, training, and mobilization. In addition to its role in military diplomacy and security of senior party and state leadership, GSD concurrently functions as Army headquarters.   GSD encompasses a large, complex bureaucracy consisting of a General Office and at least 12 second-level departments (部). Like the three other General Departments, GSD’s second-level departments and their directors are corps leader grade (正军职). Unlike the three other General Departments, however, some of the third-level bureaus (局) and their directors are corps deputy leader grade (副军职).   Over the last decade, GSD’s organizational structure has remained relatively stable with some exceptions. The most significant changes include establishment of a new department for strategic planning, and greater emphasis on informatization and joint training. GSD also has increased its role in computer network operations (CNO) and space operations. The relationship between Service Arms and Training Departments has been uncertain over the years. Although unconfirmed, GSD may be in the midst of managing a PLA reorganization, including standing up an independent Army general headquarters and military region (MR) realignment. Mark A. Stokes and Ian Easton 
 
 
Introduction 
The Chinese People’s Liberation Army (PLA) is transforming into a modern military force 
capable of an increasingly diverse set of missions further from its shores. The General Staff 
Department (GSD/总参某部/总参), which was created in March 1931, is the heart of the PLA 
and driver of its future. GSD develops policies, plans, and programs, establishes requirements, 
and allocates resources to support the PLA mission to defend the interests of the Chinese 
Communist Party (CCP). The GSD is responsible for day to day joint operations, intelligence, 
strategic planning and operational requirements, training, and mobilization. In addition to its role 
in military diplomacy and security of senior party and state leadership, GSD concurrently 
functions as Army headquarters.1  
 
GSD encompasses a large, complex bureaucracy consisting of a General Office and at least 12 
second-level departments (部). Like the three other General Departments, GSD’s second-level 
departments and their directors are corps leader grade (正军职). Unlike the three other General 
Departments, however, some of the third-level bureaus (局) and their directors are corps deputy 
leader grade (副军职).  
 
Over the last decade, GSD’s organizational structure has remained relatively stable with some 
exceptions. The most significant changes include establishment of a new department for strategic 
planning, and greater emphasis on informatization and joint training. GSD also has increased its 
role in computer network operations (CNO) and space operations. The relationship between 
Service Arms and Training Departments has been uncertain over the years. Although 
unconfirmed, GSD may be in the midst of managing a PLA reorganization, including standing 
up an independent Army general headquarters and military region (MR) realignment.2

Chapter Five: The General Political Department  Roy Kamphausen  Introduction and Overview The General Political Department (GPD/总政治部) of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) is a Central Military Commission (CMC) member–level organization (军委委员). It is led by a Director (主任) who is a member of the CMC (grade: CMC member -军委委员, rank of General) and by four deputy directors (grade of military region  正大军区职, ranging in rank from lieutenant general/vice admiral to general/admiral.)  Directors of the GPD have been members of the CMC since at least 1977 when Wei Guoqing (韦国清) became GPD Director after the end of the Cultural Revolution and the overthrow of the Gang of Four.   The GPD is responsible for the management and organization of Communist Party work within the PLA; full-spectrum personnel management; development and dissemination of Party propaganda; culture; liaison, security; legal and judicial issues; and investigations. The GPD leads the all-PLA military discipline and inspection system.   The GPD also provides the functional lead for the political commissar system found at all levels throughout the PLA, party committees at battalion and above and party branches at company and below. The GPD has three military academies that are subordinate to it, including the Nanjing Political Academy, Xi’an Political Academy, and the PLA Arts Academy (Beijing). Army Aviation Flight Group MUCD  To Katie Cannon  Although MUCDS have historically been assigned to regiment and above organizations, certain battalion leader-grade organizations have also been assigned MUCDs. Although they are battalion-level organizations, they are most likely independent battalions that are organized like and treated as a regiment. For example, several Army Aviation battalion leader-grade flight groups (飞行大队), such as the 77116 Unit in the Chengdu MR, are assigned MUCDs. Specifically, the group commander has 27 years of flight experience and is a division-deputy leader grade officer, which fits with a regiment-grade organization.[1] In addition, the group has a Political Division (政治处), which means it is a regiment-grade organization.[2]  http://szb.swpu.edu.cn/html/201011/26/20101126152828.htm. http://www.meixun.org/shuhua/shuhuayishujianshang/12626819494043.html.    http://szb.swpu.edu.cn/html/201011/26/20101126152828.htm   成都军区陆航团飞行大队大队长邓贵芳大校受邀来我校做形势与政策报告     11月25日下午，应我校政治学院之邀，成都军区陆航团飞行大队大队长，副师职特级飞行员邓贵芳大校来校做形势与政策报告。政治学院全体教职工、全校形势与政策课任课教师、部分本科生近250人在思学楼C110场聆听了报告。政治学院院长张小飞主持报告会。     邓 贵芳在报告中首先介绍了成都军区陆航团的历史沿革，从国际国内形势，从国家、民族、宗教、历史的高度，运用大量详实的数据和生动具体的实例，详解了党中央 和国务院的国防政策，深刻阐释了国家大力推进边疆经济发展、促进民族团结的战略思想，详解了成都军区陆航团在川藏航线开辟、抗震救灾、维护社会稳定、抗击 雪灾、卫星回收、“使命行动”、邱光华机组等任务。邓贵芳大校的报告既高屋建瓴、立意深远，又鲜活生动、极富感染力，为师生们上了一堂生动的形势与政策教 育课。     此次报告会是我校加强和改进形势与政策课学习的有力举措之一。有助于增加广大师生对形势与政策的了解，着力提高学习活动的针对性和规范化，促进我校形势与政策课的教学改革。     邓贵芳同志简历     现任七七一一六部队飞行大队大队长，副师职特级飞行员，男，湖北黄梅人，1964年10出生，1982年6月入伍，1987年7月入党，历任学员、飞行员、飞行大队副中队长、飞行大队中队长、飞行大队副大队长、飞行大队大队长，从事航空飞行28年，先后参加过川藏航线开辟、抗震救灾、维护社会稳定、抗击雪灾、卫星回收、“砺剑2000”、“高原2000”、“高原02”、“砺剑2005成都活动”、“使命行动-2010C”等一系列重大军事演习和多项急难险重任务，受到过胡锦涛主席的亲切接见和总部首长的高度赞扬。荣立三等功四次，二等功一次，任大队长期间，所在大队多次被评为基层建设先进单位、维护社会稳定先进单位。  www.meixun.org/shuhua/shuhuayishujianshang/12626819494043.html  邓于绘画艺术欣赏 2010年01月05日 16:47:04  来源：中国书画收藏频道  邓于先生，1957年生于天津。自幼受毕业于四川美院父母亲的影响，酷爱绘画艺术。成人后毕业于天津工业大学设计艺术学院。擅长国画人物、动物，花鸟，作品受到国内外各界收藏家的亲睐。现为四川徐悲鸿张大千艺术研究院理事长、研究院常务副院长、研究员、职业画家。 2006年11月国画作品《峥嵘岁月》选入

The PLA Army/Ground Forces Dennis J. Blasko The Chinese government has set out the general outline of its strategic intentions for the modernization of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) in its series of White Papers on national defense since 1998. Details about the PLA’s force structure, strategy, doctrine, and progress in training have been defined further in many books, essays, and articles in the Chinese military and security media, though finding and extracting relevant details often can be difficult. Additionally, continuing analysis of changes to PLA force structure and doctrine, based on new and/or more accessible Chinese sources, has added to and refined outside knowledge of recent developments. While much can be learned from a close reading of the White Papers and official Chinese media, many operational-level and tactical-level details have not been discussed fully in official sources. For example, the Chinese government still has not revealed how many personnel are assigned to each of the services nor has it stated the size of each service’s annual budget. Despite cuts of about 700,000 personnel executed in two rounds of force reductions since 1997, the PLA Army (or ground force) is estimated to remain the largest of the services, with somewhere in the range of 1.6 million personnel (although it is likely getting smaller), comprising over 60 percent of the total active-duty force. Though priority in development has been given to the Navy, Air Force, and Second Artillery, the Army also has undergone tremendous change over the past 15 years. The Army is now over 25 percent smaller in manpower with at least 25 percent fewer main force troops units than in 1997; border and coastal defense units do not appear to have undergone similar cuts. The General Armament Department (GAD), established in 1998, is the youngest of the four 
general departments. The GAD is responsible for weapons and equipment development and 
maintenance, and operating China’s test, evaluation, and training bases. The GAD also guides 
the direction of PLA modernization through its Science and Technology Committee, an 
organization with a direct channel to the CMC. 
 
The GAD was formed in April 1998 from the now defunct civilian Commission on Science, 
Technology, and Industry for National Defense (COSTIND) in an effort to establish more 
military control over weapons and equipment development and procurement. This reform 
abolished COSTIND and placed responsibility for procurement in the hands of the GAD. The 
previous procurement bureaucracy made up of COSTIND and the GLD had been characterized 
by competing interests. COSTIND, for example, was widely seen as representing the defense 
industry’s interests over the military’s interests. The new structure sought to correct this 
deficiency by centralizing control and decision-making in the hands of the newly formed GAD 
and relegated COSTIND to the diminished, but still important, role of managing the defense 
industry. 
 
The GAD-COSTIND relationship was further reformed in 2008 at the expense of COSTIND.

downgraded to the State Administration for Science, Technology, and Industry for National

The Central Military Commission (CMC) is the apex of the military hierarchy in China’s 
political order.  Under the 1982 constitution of the Chinese Communist Party (CPC), a party 
CMC is appointed by the party Central Committee at the same plenum that appoints the party 
Politburo and its Standing Committee, the Secretariat, and the Central Discipline Inspection 
Commission leadership.  Under the 1982 PRC constitution, a state CMC is appointed by the 
National People’s Congress (NPC) and is institutionally co-equal with the PRC’s executive 
branch, the State Council.  In China’s military hierarchy, the CMC is the top grade body that 
outranks every other military institution.   
 
Most studies of the CMC focus on its mission, organization and internal processes, and 
relationship with other institutions in China’s military sector.1  As critically important is this 
focus is, it nevertheless suffers from what Mao Zedong in 1929 called a “purely military 
viewpoint” that neglects the broader political context that a military serves.2  The CMC’s role, 
organization, and processes are better understood from two perspectives: first, from the 
perspective of the broader hierarchies of civilian political institutions within which it fits; and 
second, from the perspective of the military sector itself.  This paper offers assessments from 
both perspectives since 2000.

“THE PLA AND THE NEXT WAR” 
 
INTRODUCTION 
 
The Chinese People’s Liberation Army (PLA) has demonstrated over the past twenty years that it 
is a learning organization. In response to its observations and analyses of foreign wars, it has 
steadily evolved its doctrine, and then sought to modify its concepts of operations consistent with 
those doctrinal changes. 
 
Given the lack of actual combat experience on the part of the PLA, which has not fought a war 
since 1979, it is not possible to determine how well the PLA would fight in the event of an actual 
conflict. But an examination of PLA doctrinal evolution, especially its thoughts on command and 
control, may shed some light on likely PLA emphases and approaches in time of conflict.  
 
BACKGROUND 
 
In 1999, the PLA thoroughly revised its operational guidance with a new series of manuals and 
regulations that constituted the “New Generation Operations Regulations.” This marked a 
wholesale revision of operational doctrine, affecting every aspect of the PLA, from its 
conception of future wars to training and organization.  
 
The issuance of these new Regulations occurred in the context of what the PLA perceived as a 
global military transformation. This is reflected in the PLA’s view of the character of modern 
wars, which shifted from “local war under modern conditions,” i.e., industrial-era warfare, the 
PLA concluded the world was shifting towards “local war under modern, high-technology 
conditions.” Such wars are marked by several key characteristics.  
 
 The quality as well as the quantity of weapons matters. The side with more 
technologically sophisticated weapons will be able to determine the parameters of the 
conflict, and effectively control its scale and extent.

The Central Military Commission  Alice Miller Hoover Institution/Stanford University  The Central Military Commission (CMC) is the apex of the military hierarchy in China’s political order.  Under the 1982 constitution of the Chinese Communist Party (CPC), a party CMC is appointed by the party Central Committee at the same plenum that appoints the party Politburo and its Standing Committee, the Secretariat, and the Central Discipline Inspection Commission leadership.  Under the 1982 PRC constitution, a state CMC is appointed by the National People’s Congress (NPC) and is institutionally co-equal with the PRC’s executive branch, the State Council.  In China’s military hierarchy, the CMC is the top grade body that outranks every other military institution.    Most studies of the CMC focus on its mission, organization and internal processes, and relationship with other institutions in China’s military sector.  As critically important is this focus is, it nevertheless suffers from what Mao Zedong in 1929 called a “purely military viewpoint” that neglects the broader political context that a military serves.  The CMC’s role, organization, and processes are better understood from two perspectives: first, from the perspective of the broader hierarchies of civilian political institutions within which it fits; and second, from the perspective of the military sector itself.  This paper offers assessments from both perspectives since 2000.  The CMC in the Broader Political Order  The authority and place of the CMC in China’s political order today reflect the maturation of fundamental changes in the overall institutional structure and processes of politics made in the early 1980s under the leadership of Deng Xiaoping. Provided you don't have text selected, Replace All will search the entire document (all layers) and get the footnote references both in the text and in the footnotes themselves. Foreword The time period since the first PLA as Organization conference was held in 2000 and the subsequent publication of the conference volume in 2002 has seen an explosion in the number of resources, especially Internet resources, with which to analyze . With the PLA undergoing a rapid modernization and changing organizational structure to meet the demands of fighting and winning wars under informationized conditions, the ten year anniversary of the publication of the first PLA as Organization volume seemed an ideal time to take another look at the very important, but little covered topic of the PLA’s organizational structure.  From June 13-14, 2012, DGI organized a conference with some of the best minds on PLA organization issues providing their knowledge both as paper writers and conference attendees. This volume presents the outcome of that conference. The group of authors who accepted the onerous task of delving into the minutia of the PLA’s organizational structure – cataloging the numerous units and their functions, knowing the difference between a zhidui and a dadui, and, most importantly, understanding the PLA’s grades and ranks structure – spent countless hours researching their respective organizations. Their hard work has culminated in the most complete and authoritative guide to the PLA as organization since the first volume was published in 2002.  As with the first volume, this second volume is meant to be a reference guide. While some select few (and we know who you are) may be tempted to read this cover-to-cover, the vast majority of readers will gain value from this book by reading individual chapters or looking up individual organizations. With that intent, we hope that the book occupies a prominent place on people’s bookshelves as they continue their study of the PLA.  Kevin Pollpeter Kenneth W. Allen
