低空练真功
——空军哈尔滨飞行学院某旅紧贴实战组织新年度训练侧记
蓝天之上，两架战机交战正酣。新
年度首组飞行日．空军哈尔滨飞行学院
某旅就组织对抗空战、低空飞行等课目
训练，用实战标准为全年T 作开好头、
起好步。
为了确保训练安全稳妥推进．连日
来．旅里符眼训练特点，认真组织教学
研究会、飞行准备会、开飞前座舱实习
忤．准确掌握参训人员思想、心理技术
底数，扎实开展模拟特情演练和能力测
试，严把参训质量关．
刚～一开打，飞行学员凡戎杰与带敦
的飞行教官石秀亮展斤激烈对抗。陆
戎杰不断变换进攻招法．石秀亮则凭借
灵巧的技术一一摆脱。两架战机厮
杀不久，就从环空打到低空，在规定的
陈兴利
低飞行高度边缘上过招。
..'贴丑边＇的对抗．更加考验飞行
员精准的飞机控制能力和战术决策能
力。”组训人员介绍．这个时候，飞行员
既婓控制飞机保打好能扯．持纬展开
对抗．也需要时刻关注飞行高度，确保
安全。
对抗空战激烈，低空训练也紧张展
开，为了练硬飞行学员实战能力，飞行
教官穆亚南结合实战标准给学员划出
三条硬杠杠：一是严格按照标准快速下
降；二是飞出大纲明确的聂低高度；二
是低空转弯动作要丝屯不差。一条标
准没达到，就是樊次不及格．重飞！
“低空训练只有坚持质量标准，才
能保证安全、提高效益。＂穆亚南告诉笔
者，他带教的一名学员平时爱动脑．经
常有新发现。他坚持因材施教．让这名
学员严格按标准要求实施。这天，这名
学员不仅低空训练飞得好，而且和飞行
教＇臼验证了多项内容
”这次飞书不错．按要求顺利完成
了任务。“飞行归来．穆亚南首先对这名
学员的表现给予甘定，紧接苍就讲起了
训练中的不足，他边回放架次视频．边
结合实战进行讲评：“低空突击除了要
把握好高度，还要严格把控时间窃U 。
这次攻击的时间就在窗口结束前
1 秒．
差一点就无法完成任务，我们追求的必
须是精准制杜！“这名学员听后表示．对
下一个架次的飞行有f更高的标准、吏
泊晰的认识。
星夜强铁翼
一空军西安飞行学院某旅从难从严组织夜航训练侧记
1 月上旬，大漠究风刺骨，足光闪
烁空军西安飞行学院某旅机场上，飞
行学员石彪打行手电简，从前起落架
开始检查。他仔细端详心、触摸符、戏
视芷......这是他飞行以来养成的－·个＇
习惯。
当天，是该旅新年度白次夜航训
练，石彪则是夜航首飞的学从。虽然
前期训练扎实、经过带飞检查，但他丝
谥不敢大意，严格按要求做好飞行前
的准备。
与此同时，保障官兵也在抓紧准备
飞机，装尾伞、充氧气、连接电桥、通电
检查..'．．．他们密切协作，认具作业，确
保每一步都做到位。
橐建伟翟夭毅
超介绍．旅里严格洛实大纲要求．坚持按
t限组训
开训前飞行大队通过开展
教宁研究会、飞行准备会、座舱实习等．
准确掌拟参训人员思想、心理．技术底
数．确代安全托底。同时．他们详细梳理
女全风险点，还一制定预防措施，区分不
同课H 组织模拟特情颅练和开飞前测
试，严把开飞准备朊量关
”计时起飞！”转眼间．指挥吊下达
起飞指令二七彪推油门拉杆......只见
战鹰在夜色中呼啸者奔赴目标空城。
随右飞机升空，机舱内凸渐变暗．最后
仅剩下仪表盘发出的微弱光亮，透过
飞机佐窗向下望去，大地漆里如员。石
彪通过目视和仪表设备来判定飞机的
飞行姿态和位五，准确把担航速航线。
茫茫夜空．他按照地面协同准备内容熟
练橾纵战机，顺利完成所有训练动作．
聂终稳稳丑陆
“机动果敢泼辣，但保持空中飞行
姿态还需稳定·比。“下了飞机．石彪和
飞行教官徐亮直奔塔台，心过飞参判
读、视频回放．对空中实施｝习况进行全
面复盘研究。
练兵毫不懈怠，备战不分朝夕。又
一架战咸向着夜空飞去，而陆续归航的
学员们难掩兴奋之情．热烈讨论交流
夜航训练体会。
KC(15 
“虽然夜航训练组织指挥较为复杂、
危险系数比较高，但我们坚持开年就组
织该课目训练，给予牛员更多放手邕，着
力立起实战实训的导向，“飞行教官千英
大抓实战化军事训练
练就能战善战的精兵劲旅
打好安全工作”组合拳”
西部战区空军雷达某旅抓好节前部队安全管理
本报讯范云龙报道加强与驻
地公安、网监等郘17 联防联治；对灭火
器．消防沙等安全设施进行更新维护；
制定军人驾乘私家车管控措施......仕
节前夕．西部战区空军雷达某旅前移安
全工作关口．健全烦防机制，进一步筑
牢安全防范基础。
该旅党委从找准薄弱环节、解决倾
向性问题入手．着力消除安全总患己｛也
们组织开展安全警示教育．引导官兵从
典型华故

开栏的话
当时间的齿轮轻轻转动，人民空军
满怀信心奋飞在新航程上。
务实举措和丰硕成果，展现空军官兵坚守战位、枕戈
待旦，聚焦实现建军一百年奋斗目标，奋力开创人民
空军现代化新局面的崭新风貌
从今天起，本报开
设“新春走军营”专栏，陆续刊发前方记者发回的报
道。敬请关注！
临近春节，本报记者和特约记者兵分多路再度
出发，走向一线战位，走近基层官兵，反映空军部队
用党的二十大精神武装头脑、指导实践、推动工作的
空军通信某旅机动通信分队自主选题研题提升信息支撑能力
无形战场满眼“新”
赵蔚光本报记者王日特约记者郭海洋
年关将至．年味愈浓，战味也愈
浓。
1 月9 日．伴壮阵阵寒风．记者来
到空军通信某旅机动通信分队营
区。只见训练场上．多型机动通信装
备列阵展开．官兵们密切协同操作、
创新战法训法，一过这电波跨赵千里
联通千军万马
某甘营长吕俊晓告诉记者．其实
机动通信分队并非建制单位，而是担
负机动通信任务让连的统称．可以依
据任务特点灵活编组，以适应未来战
场需要。记者跟随吕营长米到某指
扞方舱，旁观了一场紧张的“战斗”。
只见红方通估链路遭蓝方打击中易气，
前出的战机噬即英去了成场数据
面对特情．指挥员与项航员迅速研
判，果断应用前期的创新成果，在最
短时间内恢复了信道构建，为战机准
确命中靶标提供了态势支撑
“党的一寸十大报告指出，创新是
第－动力。提高打赢能力，就必须适
应现代战争规律特点．主动求新、求
变。“吕营长介绍，这几年他们取得不
少创新成果，机动分队将作战要素单
元摸块化嵌入指挥链条织网固网．在
多种不同作战样式背费下开展组网联
试、抗毁联动等高难度课目训练．有6
项战法创新成果仕空军部队推广。
特别是去年，为了提升快速通信
支援能力，该旅主动向上级提出采取
空转协运漠式前出的构想。此后，在
航空兵某师的配合下，装备和人员被
快速投送到｀战场”。“以往铁路机动
需要数天．这次不到3 个小时，而且
官兵们没有了长途劳顿，精力更加充
沛！“吕营长说。
新年更有新”气象．年初以来．
为推进战法运用与日常训练探度耦
合，该旅邢笘开展战法训法研练沽
动。机动通信分队积极受顷任务．图
绕制约战斗力生成的瓶颈自主选题
开题结合日常训练进行研究驼证。
“我们打算拓帛｀
－专多能＇计
划，“吕营长和记者分亨了他们的新
计划。去年参加一次重大清习时，官
兵们分成多个小队．机动部署、构达
指挥小体系效灵不错，可任务钻束
后，大家都提到一个问题，炊事员不
够．虽然有自执食品，但任务时间跨
度长，如果有炊事保障，官兵们的状
态会更好。
吕营长为记者算了一笔账．一个
小队十几个人，全少有3 台车，如果
驾驶员会做饭，问题就迎刃而解了。
所以他们一季度挑选了5 名驾驶员
进行炊事技能培训，顺利的话到今
年年底，就可以实现每个小队都有自
己的炊事员。此前，他们已经培养了
很多”一专多能”型人才．全营有超过
20％的台站长由装备驾驶员兼任。
“除了完成营里的课题，我们连
也有自己的想法。＂某连连长周煜皓
兴致勃勃地告诉记者，去年的一项演
训任务中，他们采用常规的卫星组网
颠利完成任务。但年初的课题研讨
会上，有人提出，老方法虽然简单，能
满足当前任务需求，但如果未夹遇到
更大容量的指挥需求．容易发生网络
拥堵。
“我们今年要尝试的新模式，虽
然操作起来复杂很多，但优势是占用
频谱资桥小、抗干扰能力更强．能够
适应更复杂的战场环境。“周煜站说．
新课题的研究得到了大家的积极响
应，当务之急是加快人员能力升级，
第一批争取为每个台站培养·一名熟
练掌握新模式的业务付于．之后辐射
到全连。
“党的二十大蚊画了宏伟蓝出，
面对新形势新使命，只有主动出击．
不断掌握新技能、增长新本领．才能
打赢未来战争．＂该旅领导告诉记者，
全旅官兵认真学习贯彻党的－．十大
精神，新年度奋进目标更加清晰．就
是要把全部精力向打仗殁焦，全部工
作向打仗用劲，不断增强实战化条件
下的信息支撑能力。
目送着机动通位分队车队呼啸
而过．记者坚信，在如期实现建军一
百年奋斗目标的新征程上，这支信
息通信尖兵定能经略无形战场、制
胜高远空天，谱写红色传令兵新的
荣光．
KDOl 
凝聚合力防治酒驾醉驾
近期，空军首长组织防治石后为车
问题专超研究，指出酒后驾车问题已经
成为危及安全稳定的立要因素，娄求各
级必须真正形成共管合力
防治，目后芷
车问题是一项系统工才1 ，常妻机关与基
层合力、镇导和官兵共

实方法对接”活思想”
空后某直属保障大队扎实开展经常性思想教育纪实
在学习贯彻党的二十大精神中，官
兵纷纷上台谈领悟、话体会；紧盯岁末
年初工作特点，机关及时为在外人员及
其家属转发洒驾、季节性安全事故预防
等提示奄，．…新年伊始，这是记者在空后
某直属保障大队看到的一幕样。
近年来，该大队党委聚焦官兵”活
思想＂待续发力，狠抓经常性思想教育．
及时解疑释惑，开展正向引导，创新教
育形式，让时代”主旋律”、社会“正能
批“和军营“好声音”牢牢占据官兵思想
阵地。
既做好”大锅
饭”，又精炒“小锅菜”
该大队所属单位及人员类型多样，
既有以普通官兵为主的基层部队，也有
以专业技术军官和文职人员为主的业务
机构．还有以职工和社聘人员居多的保
障力社。面对营区点位分散、保陓任务
繁重，入员层次复杂等特点，如何统筹抓
好思想教育工作成为．道”必答题”
而最初，这道”必答题“难住了不少
人。该大队某保障室编制上没有配设
专职政治干部，“清一色”的专业技术军
官中懂教育会施教的屈指可数。为了
强化施教者能力素质，他们通过组织基
层党支部书记培训、开展思想及心理骨
干队伍梊中培训和政治教员大比武等
系列活动，不断锤炼提升干部骨干”会
知兵、会施教、会谈心、身教好”的能
力。同时，常态开展”书香满军营“群众
性读书活动，引导官兵多读书、读好书，
发挥优秀图书滋养心灵、陶冶情操的育
人功能
该大队政1台工作部领导还带
队深入所屈各单位开展调查研究，了解
人员思想动态，听取基层意见建议，力
求使教百｀供给侧“精准对接”需求侧＂
刘钊特约记者许森
增强教育的实效性，既要做好”大
锅饭”，又要精炒＂小锅菜＇。该大队研
究制定（思想政冶教育四个全寝盛”规
范措施）．区分机关、业务机构等单位，
制定精准施教指南．细化教育计划，对
军官、战士、文职人员、职工和社聘人员
逐一规范教育内容，明确方法要求。提
起这个措施．不少思想教育工作骨干直
言：＂施教者一看就能懂、照茬就会做，
开展教育授课时更有底气了。”
人人上讲台，个
个当主角

Lt Gen Cai Yingting’s Talking Points On 24 August Lt Gen Cai Yingting and his delegation of 11 other officers attended a roundtable discussion at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) in Washington, DC. He spoke for about 25-30 minutes and then opened the floor for questions. Throughout his presentation and in answering each question, he provided a numbered list of talking points. Cai is the newest member of the PLA’s six deputy chiefs of the general staff (DCOGS), having assumed his position in July 2011. Although his grade (military region leader) will remain the same, he will eventually receive his third star (full general) in about two years. Although it is was not clear if he spoke English, he did look at his interpreter a few times to clarify some points and corrected him a couple of times, which indicates he speaks at least some English. Although his portfolio does not include foreign relations, which belongs to DCGOS General Ma Xiaotian, he spoke with authority and ease throughout the presentation. Although he used talking points throughout the discussion, he said “I assess” multiple times rather than saying the PLA or Central Military Commission (CMC) or President Hu Jin Tao assesses. Based on a discussion with a participant at Cai’s follow-on meeting at National Defense University, he did not use numbered talking points during that meeting. US-China Military Relations and Strategy During his presentation, he noted the following points concerning US-China military relations. 1. Strategic thinking: Relations between an established and rising power should be based on a creative strategic thinking. 2. Strategic assessments: What strategic assessments should each side make concerning the other and concerning their relationship? Cai bases his assessment on the need for communications between both sides because both sides need each other if peace is to continue. 3. Overview (Draft) April 2014 – March 2015  This overview addresses some of the major issues and changes related to personnel and organizations in China’s armed forces that occurred over the past year. The first section addresses the structural ramifications of the nationwide anticorruption drive and targeted efforts within the military to strengthen fiscal oversight, increase accountability, and improve morale among officers and men. The second section highlights recent high-level promotions in the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) and People’s Armed Police (PAP). The third section analyzes several important documents and statements related to Chinese military doctrine that were issued early in 2015.   I. Corruption and LSGs Over the past year, one of the most prominent and persistent themes within the Chinese military from both an organizational and political perspective has been the localized effects of the nationwide anti-corruption campaign led by Chinese President and Central Military Commission (CMC) Chairman Xi Jinping [习近平]. Under Xi, the lead man for anti-corruption work in China is Chinese Communist Party (CCP) Politburo Standing Committee Member Wang Qishan [王岐山], who serves as Secretary-General of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI). The Secretary of the subordinate CMC Discipline Inspection Committee is Du Jincai [杜金才], who is also Deputy Director (DDIR) of the General Political Department (GPD). In 2014, discipline inspection efforts conducted throughout China led to the investigation of 28 officials at the provincial or ministerial level and above, including former Minster of Public Security Zhou Yongkang [周永康] and former CMC Vice Chairman (VC) Xu Caihou [徐才厚]. In a work report delivered at the Third Session of the 12th National People’s Congress, the Supreme People’s Procuratorate announced that in 2014, 55,101 people were investigated in a total of 41,187 cases. The total number of people investigated increased 7.4 percent from the p

Overview 
April 2016 – March 2017 
This overview will seek to provide a summary of significant personnel and organizational changes to 
China’s armed forces in the period of April 2016 – March 2017. In general, when comparing this period 
to the previous year (April 2015 – March 2016), reporting on national defense and military reforms has 
seen a shift in emphasis from high-level organizations, notably the revamped Central Military 
Commission (CMC) and theater commands that were created in early 2016, to an emphasis on reforms in 
mid- and lower-level organizations.  
Official military media reports, observed in sources such as Jiefangjun Bao (PLA Daily) [解放军报], 
have described the difference between high- and lower-level reforms as “above the neck” [脖子以上] and 
“below the neck” [脖子以下]. In September 2015, a PLA academic defined “above the neck” reforms as 
those related to the optimization of the military’s leadership and command system [领导指挥体制].1 In 
March 2016, following the creation of the new CMC organization with 15 functional departments, 
Jiefangjun Bao declared that “above the neck” reforms were “showing good results.”2 The exact nature of 
“below the neck” reforms has been less well defined, though they are known to concern force 
reorganization involving the removal and relocation of operational units as well as personnel cuts. By 
mid-December 2016, days after a CMC meeting on force reorganization,3 “below the neck” reforms were 
confirmed to be underway,4 and in early 2017, Jiefangjun Bao carried numerous reports of regiment-level 
units being resubordinated. 
This overview will address the following three topics: the founding of the Joint Logistic Support Force 
(JLSF), significant personnel changes, and other organizational changes.  
 
I. The Founding of the Joint Logistic Support Force 
Over the past year, the most significant organization to be created was the CMC JLSF [中央军委联勤保
障部队].5 An establishment ceremony for the new force was held on 13

filename: PLA and PAP Grade and Rank Structure - Modification_KA Edits.docx
text_snippet:
PLA and PAP Grade and Rank Structure
 As a starting point, it is important to understand that officer (cadre) grades, not ranks, are the key to understanding the PLA’s organizational structure. Since 1988, the PLA has had 10 officer ranks and 15 grades. The PLA and PAP assign grades not only to officers and billets, but also to every PLA organization, including naval vessels commanded by officers but not noncommissioned officers (NCOs). This system, which all PLA services and branches use, is based on ground force terminology. There are four key differences between the U.S. military and the PLA in terms of their use of rank and grade: First and most importantly, in the PLA, rank is not as important as grade. Rank insignia are used primarily as a visual cue to rapidly determine where one’s approximate status is within a group and for interacting with foreign militaries.  Second, in the PLA, billets are assigned based on one’s grade, not rank. Third, promotion in grade, not rank, is what determines how one moves up the career ladder. For example, moving from senior colonel to major general while remaining in the same grade is not as important as moving from one grade to the next, even if you retain the same rank.  Finally, every PLA organization, not just officers and billets, is assigned a grade. Therefore, it is the grade system that defines the vertical command and control (C2) and horizontal coordination relationship among organizations. 
In the wake of the PLA reforms that started on 1 January 2016, much is unknown about how the grade and rank structure will also change.  Nonetheless, we either know or can anticipate the following changes:  The Military Region (正大军区职) and Deputy Military Region grade (副大军区职) will most likely be renamed to Theater Command (正战区职 or 正大战区职) and, possibly, Deputy Theater Command grade (副战区职 OR 副大战区职).  At the time of this publication, we have not seen any evidence of this change; however, with the removal of the Military Region system

2016 Directory Issues 30 June 2016  Good Afternoon,  I have put on my best version of a Ken Allen mind-hat and attempted to update the PLA and PAP Grade and Rank Document (see attached).  I haven't had the chance to make it all the way through and stopped just after tweaking Table 5: Deputy Military Region Grade (TC Service HQs + Fleets and others) but wanted to give you all a chance to review it before I go any further.  I must say, after living and breathing the grade and rank system for the past four years, I found this exercise extremely helpful and, to my surprise, easy.  Just goes to prove that the system is a really good one and well presented by Ken.  So far, I have run into the following questions:  1. Prior to Table 3, is there a need for describing the CMC’s first level 6 Departments (部), 3 Commissions (委员会), 5 Offices (办公室, 署), 1 Agency (总局)?  Also difference in grade between them all? 2. Is there a need to acknowledge the presence of Support Departments in DIVs/BDEs/RGTs in the Introduction of the document? 3. Prior to Table 4 and 5, is there a need for describing TCs as having Staff D, PWD, Joint Ops Command Center and Service HQs in further detail? 4. Is there a need for describing the PLAA as having its own Commander/PC now in the beginning part of this document?  Same thing for the PLASSF? 5. Lastly, one of the more positive outcomes of revising this document was that I noticed I had assigned the second-level bureaus for the CMC Training and Administration Department - 训练管理部 and National Defense Mobilization Department - 国防动员部 (both MR Deputy Leader grade orgs) a Corps-leader grade.  Shouldn't they be a Corps Deputy Leader grade?  I looked over the personnel we do have identified in these second-level ureau positions and, previously, they all held Corps Deputy ;eader grades.  I'm inclined to change it as exhibited in Table 3.  A big thank you again to Ken for teaching me this system over the years and letting me try my hand at this/work of the docs

filename: Grade Change Documentation for Senior PLA Organizations_KA Edits.docx
text_snippet: Grade Change Documentation for Senior PLA Organizations I believe that grade changes for the CMC’s Joint Staff Department and Political Work Department and the Academy of Military Science, National Defense University, and National University of Defense Technology happened as early as July 2017 and maybe even as early as January/February 2017.  19 July 2017 is an interesting date for a formal downgrade because that is when Xi conferred flags to the "newly reorganized" AMS , NDU, and NUDT (http://www.xinhuanet.com/politics/2017-07/19/c_1121347127.htm). I always thought that event was odd because these organizations already existed, but, looking back, that event may have been held, among other reasons, to signify they were a reorganized (i.e. downgraded in this example) organization.  Of note, since 19 July 2019, all personnel who have been assigned to deputy of assistant billets in these organizations previously held a Grade 4, 5, or 6 position.  If the old grade structure was still in place, then we should have seen someone transferring from a Grade 3 (for deputy billets) or Grade 4 (for assistant billets) position.  That has not happened.  I mention that it may have happened even earlier than then because, if the JSD hadn’t been downgraded, then Shao Yuanming would have had to skip a grade (always possible) to hold his current position as Deputy Chief, JSD (formerly a Grade 3 position).  *=Personnel would have had to skip a grade if the organization has yet to be downgraded  Joint Staff Department Presumed to now be a TC Leader (3) grade organization  Deputy Chiefs (Presumed Grade 4 Billet) Ma Yiming [马宜明] – Former Assistant to the Chief, JSD (Grade 4) Shao Yuanming [邵元明] – Former Deputy Chief of Staff, PLARF (Grade 5)* Chang Dingqiu [常丁求] – Former Deputy Commander, STC (Grade 4)  Assistant to the Chief (Presumed Grade 5 Billet) Jiang Guoqing [姜国平] – Former Chief of Staff, North Sea Fleet (NTC Navy) (Grade 5) Chen Guangjun [陈光军] – Former Director, Training Departmen

Opening Comments Commissioners, for someone who has been involved in observing and analyzing People’s Liberation Army (PLA) personnel issues for over 30 years, it is an honor to be invited to present my views on this important topic to the commission.  Today, I will address personnel issues organized into 3 components: First, the two-year conscript force; Second, the non-commissioned officer (NCO) corps; and third, the officer corps.  Although the focus of the report is on the current situation, it is important to understand how the PLA got to where it is today, so I will begin by providing a very brief overview of the situation since 1999, including the current force size. I will then discuss the current situation.  In my opinion, the PLA’s personnel continue to be the weak link, and we can expect even more changes over the next decade to address this issue. Key Findings The PLA has continued to make major adjustments to its enlisted force since 1999.These include creating a 30-year enlisted force, recruiting college students and graduates as two-year conscripts, shifting from a one-cycle to a two-cycle per year conscription system in 2021, and directly recruiting personnel with special technical skills as NCOs.  The turnover of conscripts each year affects the annual training cycle, such that units are missing a significant number of personnel for months at a time. text_snippet: Overview of English-language Sources on the PLA Kenneth W. Allen This paper provides an overview about English-language sources that provide analysis of the People’s Republic of China’s (PRC) People’s Liberation Army (PLA) since the 1960s. The information can be divided into the following categories: 1) conferences, 2) books, 3) reports, and 4) articles. Some of the categories overlap, such as books or reports written based on papers presented at a conference. Other books are written by a single author on a single topic, such as M. Taylor Fravel’s Active Defense: China’s Military Strategy since 1949 and Dennis J. Blasko’s The Chinese Army Today: Tradition and Transformation for the 21st Century (Asian Security Studies) 2nd Edition. As a general rule, almost every author is a Chinese linguist and the books, reports, and articles are based on original Chinese sources. Given the amount of information available, this paper is not all inclusive but provides a baseline for trying to understand the big picture. Furthermore, although the paper does not provide a list of authors, there is a core of analysts who have been writing about the PLA for a long time. Based on analysis of the books identified in the report, about 75% of them can be purchased from Amazon and a lot of them can be downloaded for free from various websites. The report is organized into the following 6 sections and 6 appendices Section 1: Conferences and Related Publications Section 2: U.S. Government Publications Section 3: Key Websites  Section 4: Foreign Publications Section 5: PRC Defense White Papers and MND Website Section 6: Taiwan National Defense Reports Appendix A: Staunton Hill to Carlisle Conference Publications Appendix B: NBR Conferences Appendix C: CAPS Conferences Appendix D: DOD Annual Report to Congress Appendix E: PLA-related Books and Publications Appendix F: Acronyms Section 1: PLA Conferences This section provides an overview and background material for several PLA conferences dating

Which one of the 15 CMC organizations is listed first in protocol order and what is its grade?  What organization is replacing the former Logistics Department and Equipment Department at the Theater Command Service HQ and below? How many officer academic institutions are there?  What is the current structure for academic institutions?  Do all officers receive a bachelor’s degree? Are commanders and political officers still co-equals if they do not have the same rank? What types of Party Committees are there?  Who makes up the Party Standing Committee in a unit, and who is the secretary and deputy secretary? How many deputy commanders do units have? What is the role of the chief of staff?  How many years do enlisted conscripts/new recruits serve? How many NCO grade levels and ranks are there Was Second Artillery a service or a branch? Is the Strategic Support Force a service? Is there a central promotion board for all personnel?  Do personnel have to live on base? How many Theater Commands (TCs) and Theater Command Air Forces (TCAFs) are there and what is their protocol order?  Does the PLA Navy still have fleets? What is the grade assigned to each of the 5  TCAF’s and TC Navies (Northern, Eastern, and Southern)?  Do the PLA’s Ministry of National Defense and U.S. Department of Defense have the same basic responsibilities?     Answers What concurrent positions do the 4 members of the Central Military Commission (CMC) hold? Minister of National Defense; Chief of the Joint Staff Department (JSD); Director, Political Work Department (PWD); Secretary, Discipline Inspection Commission (DIC) What are the officer career tracks? Re-Engaging With the World
China's Military Diplomacy in 2023 
April 2024
Jie Gao and Kenneth W. Allen
 Summary 
China’s military diplomacy plays a crucial role in advancing the nation's foreign policy objectives 
and safeguarding its strategic interests. This report highlights a diverse array of activities within 
military diplomacy, including senior-level meetings, joint military exercises, naval port calls, UN 
peacekeeping operations, and academic exchanges. Our findings reveal a significant—but 
incomplete—recovery in China's military diplomacy activities in 2023, following a period of reduced 
contacts with foreign militaries during the COVID-19 pandemic. Southeast Asia and Russia remain 
primary partners for China, with emerging strategic importance also seen in Africa, Oceania, and the 
Middle East. Overall, China's military diplomacy underscores its efforts to diversify partnerships and 
assert its global influence, emphasizing regional stability and international cooperation in pursuit of 
its diplomatic goals. 
Introduction 
During their San Francisco meeting in November 2023, Chinese and U.S. statesmen agreed to 
resume high-level military-to-military communications, paving the way for the first talks between 
their top defense officials in over a year.1 While China almost froze its military diplomacy with the 
United States in 2023, the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) was busy enhancing its cooperation with 
other foreign militaries. According to the PLA, “military diplomacy is an important component of a 
country’s foreign affairs, and it can even be considered the ‘ballast stone’(压舱石) of a nation’s 
diplomacy.”2 Thus, tracking the PLA’s diplomatic activities can help us better understand trends in 
China’s overall diplomatic goals and interests.

Summary China’s military diplomacy plays a crucial role in advancing the nation's foreign policy objectives and safeguarding its strategic interests. The report highlights a diverse array of activities within military diplomacy, including senior-level meetings, joint military exercises, naval port calls, UN peacekeeping operations, and academic exchanges. Our findings reveal a significant recovery in China's military diplomacy activities in 2023, following a period of reduced contacts with foreign militaries. Southeast Asia and Russia remain primary partners for China, with emerging strategic importance also seen in Africa, Oceania, and the Middle East. Overall, China's military diplomacy underscores its efforts to diversify partnerships and assert its global influence, emphasizing regional stability and international cooperation in pursuit of its diplomatic goals. Introduction During their San Francisco meeting in November 2023, Chinese and U.S. statesmen agreed to resume high-level military-to-military communications, paving the way to the first talk between their top defense officials in over a year. While China almost froze its military diplomacy with the United States in 2023, the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) was busy enhancing its cooperation with other foreign militaries. According to the PLA, “military diplomacy is an important component of a country’s foreign affairs, and it can even be considered the ‘ballast stone’(压舱石) of a nation’s diplomacy.” Thus, tracking the PLA’s diplomatic activities can help us better understand trends in China’s overall diplomatic goals and interests.  This report will review China’s military diplomacy activities in 2023, including senior-level visits and meetings, joint exercises, naval port calls, and humanitarian activities. important to see what the PLAAF is saying to the international community about itself.1 How does 
it present itself in terms of its capabilities and missions, and what does it choose to signal about 
its intentions? One of the PLAAF’s key channels for conveying its messages to an international 
audience is the Ministry of National Defense’s (MND) China Military Online website. It is the only 
official English language military news website of the Chinese Armed Forces, and describes itself 
as “an important platform for building up the online international communication capacity of the 
Chinese military.”2 China Military Online provides news stories and photos about the PLAAF in its 
own section of the website, with content currently going back to 2018.3 These are the stories that 
the Chinese air force wants to share with the international community about who it is and what it 
does.4  
This report offers a close examination of what the Chinese air force has been saying to the 
international community, describing the content of the 169 articles about the PLAAF that were 
published between January 2018 and August 2023 on the China Military Online website.5 While 
most reports on the PLAAF rely on multiple sources and tend to focus on a single topic, such as 
policy, strategy, or training during a certain period, this report focuses on a single source and what 
it is communicating. The PLAAF articles that appear on China Military Online are both original to 
the site and adapted content that originally appeared in other Chinese media, such as Xinhuanet, 
Global Times (which is part of People’s Daily), and China Daily. Although some of these sources 
are not military organizations, the reporters and editor for each article on China Military Online 
are military officers.

Abstract  This report conducts an in-depth assessment of various personnel-related topics in the People’s Liberation Army (PLA), as requested by the U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission (USCC). Information is drawn primarily from open-source research and analysis of Chinese documents, regulations, newspaper articles, and books, as well as from authoritative secondary sources. Topics include PLA personnel quality and shortfalls, challenges the PLA faces in improving personnel quality, recruiting and retention of quality personnel, desired education and expertise, conscription vs. voluntary recruitment, morale, combat readiness, personnel socio-economic makeup, and the role of politics in the PLA.  Among the key findings, this report suggests that Xi Jinping has continued doubts about personnel competence and loyalty since becoming the Chairman of the Central Military Commission in 2012, and thus has focused on both force modernization and Party loyalty.
